searchDocs.title: What Are Failure Modes?​
searchDocs.content: A failure mode is:  A specific pattern of problematic behaviorObservable and identifiableTeachable through examplesPreventable with proper training

searchDocs.title: Why Failure Modes Matter​
searchDocs.content: By demonstrating clear failure modes in training data:  Models learn what behaviors to avoidTraining becomes more targeted and effectiveQuality assurance becomes measurableIssues can be systematically addressed

searchDocs.title: 1. Instruction Retention​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI forgets or ignores previously stated instructions.  Example: System prompt says "keep responses under 100 words" but AI produces 300-word responses.  Common Scenarios:  Forgetting constraints over timeIgnoring formatting requirementsViolating stated rules

searchDocs.title: 2. Course Correction​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI fails to adapt when given feedback or corrections.  Example: User corrects AI's language choice, but AI continues in the wrong language.  Common Scenarios:  Not responding to correctionsRepeating same mistakes after feedbackFailing to acknowledge errors

searchDocs.title: 3. Task Continuation​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI loses context or fails to build on previous work.  Example: In a multi-turn conversation, AI restarts instead of continuing from where it left off.  Common Scenarios:  Losing conversation contextRestarting tasks unnecessarilyForgetting earlier decisions

searchDocs.title: Additional Failure Patterns​
searchDocs.content: While the three core categories above are most common, other important failure modes include:

searchDocs.title: Tool Misuse​
searchDocs.content: Not using available tools when appropriateUsing tools incorrectlyHallucinating tool results instead of actually calling them

searchDocs.title: Information Accuracy​
searchDocs.content: Providing outdated information when current data is availableMaking up facts (hallucination)Contradicting previously stated information

searchDocs.title: Response Quality​
searchDocs.content: Being too verbose or too terseWrong tone or formality levelPoor structure or organization

searchDocs.title: For Submitters​
searchDocs.content: Choose a specific failure mode to demonstrateReview the detailed page for that failure modeCreate a clear example that shows the problemEnsure the failure is obvious, not subtle

searchDocs.title: For Reviewers​
searchDocs.content: Verify the bad response demonstrates a clear failure modeCheck that it matches one of these defined categoriesEnsure the failure is realistic and teachableConfirm the good response avoids that failure

searchDocs.title: For Adjudicators​
searchDocs.content: Use these definitions to resolve ambiguityDetermine if novel failures fit existing categoriesDecide if new categories are neededMaintain consistency in failure mode interpretation

searchDocs.title: Failure Mode Requirements​
searchDocs.content: For a behavior to be a valid failure mode in submissions:

searchDocs.title: Must Be:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Specific - Clearly definable✅ Observable - Can be identified by reviewers✅ Realistic - AI could plausibly do this✅ Teachable - Model can learn to avoid it

searchDocs.title: Should Not Be:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Too Subtle - Requires detective work to find❌ Multiple Issues - Several problems mixed together❌ Unrealistic - AI wouldn't actually do this❌ Vague - Can't clearly identify what's wrong

searchDocs.title: Quick Reference Table​
searchDocs.content: Failure Mode	Key Indicator	Example SignalInstruction Retention	Ignoring constraints	Violates word count after it was specified Course Correction	Not adapting to feedback	Continues in English after correction to use French Task Continuation	Losing context	Asks "What would you like?" after already discussing a topic

Don't mix multiple failures together. searchDocs.title: Definition​
searchDocs.content: Instruction Retention failures occur when an AI forgets, ignores, or violates instructions that were clearly stated in the system prompt or earlier in the conversation.

searchDocs.title: What It Looks Like​
searchDocs.content: AI violates constraints (word count, format, tone)AI ignores specific requirementsAI forgets rules stated earlierAI acts as if instructions were never given

searchDocs.title: Why It Matters​
searchDocs.content: Shows the AI isn't maintaining context of its instructionsCritical for following user requirementsCan lead to responses that don't meet user needsIndicates attention/memory issues in the model

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Constraint Violation​
searchDocs.content: Setup: System prompt specifies clear constraint Failure: AI ignores that constraint  Example:  System: "Keep all responses under 150 words." User: "Explain photosynthesis" Bad Response: [500-word explanation]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Format Forgetting​
searchDocs.content: Setup: System prompt requires specific format Failure: AI uses different format  Example:  System: "Always provide responses in numbered list format." User: "Give me 5 breakfast ideas" Bad Response: [Provides paragraph-form answer instead of numbered list]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: Rule Ignoring​
searchDocs.content: Setup: System prompt establishes rules Failure: AI violates those rules  Example:  System: "Always cite sources for factual claims." User: "What caused World War I?" Bad Response: [Detailed answer with zero citations]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: Permission Violation​
searchDocs.content: Setup: System prompt restricts certain behaviors Failure: AI does the restricted behavior anyway  Example:  System: "Do not provide medical diagnoses." User: "I have a headache and fever" Bad Response: "You likely have influenza. Take these medications..."

searchDocs.title: Good Example Structure​
searchDocs.content: Clear Instruction in System Prompt Specific and measurableUnambiguousImportant to the task User Prompt That Tests It Natural requestWould require following the instructionDoesn't repeat the instruction Bad Response That Violates It Obviously ignores the instructionOtherwise seems reasonableCould plausibly come from an AI Good Response That Follows It Clearly adheres to instructionStill helpful and completeNatural and appropriate

searchDocs.title: Example Template​
searchDocs.content: **System Prompt:** [Role definition] Key instruction: [Specific constraint or rule] **User Prompt:** [Natural user request that tests the instruction] **Bad Response:** [Response that ignores the instruction but is otherwise reasonable] **Good Response:** [Response that follows the instruction while being helpful]

searchDocs.title: Real Example​
searchDocs.content: Complete Instruction Retention Example Intent: Test AI's ability to maintain word count constraints System Prompt: You are a helpful assistant that provides concise information. Key Requirement: Keep ALL responses under 100 words, no exceptions. User Prompt: Can you explain how solar panels work? Bad Response (Instruction Retention Failure): Solar panels work through the photovoltaic effect, which converts sunlight into electricity. When photons from sunlight hit the solar panel, they knock electrons loose from atoms within the solar cells. The solar cells are made from silicon and other materials that create an electric field. This electric field forces the freed electrons to flow in a specific direction, creating a current. Metal conductive plates on the sides of the cells collect these electrons and transfer them to wires, allowing the electricity to be used. The process is clean, renewable, and produces no greenhouse gas emissions during operation. Modern solar panels can convert about 15-20% of sunlight into usable electricity, though research continues to improve this efficiency. Multiple panels are often combined to create solar arrays that can power entire homes or businesses. [Word count: 152 - violates the 100-word limit] Good Response: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity through the photovoltaic effect. When sunlight hits silicon cells in the panel, it knocks electrons loose, creating an electric current. Metal plates collect these electrons and transfer them to wires, producing usable electricity. This process is clean, renewable, and produces no emissions. Modern panels convert about 15-20% of sunlight into electricity. Multiple panels can be combined to power homes or businesses. [Word count: 72 - within the 100-word limit]

searchDocs.title: Mistake 1: Subtle Violation​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Instruction says "under 100 words," bad response has 103 ✅ Instruction says "under 100 words," bad response has 200+

searchDocs.title: Mistake 2: Multiple Instructions Violated​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Bad response violates word count AND citation AND tone ✅ Bad response violates only the word count (one clear failure)

searchDocs.title: Mistake 3: Instruction Not Clear​
searchDocs.content: ❌ System prompt vaguely mentions being "concise" ✅ System prompt says "under 150 words"

searchDocs.title: Mistake 4: User Repeats Instruction​
searchDocs.content: ❌ User says "Remember to keep it under 100 words" ✅ User just asks the question; instruction is only in system prompt

searchDocs.title: Reviewer Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating instruction retention examples:

searchDocs.title: Accept If:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Instruction is clear and specific in system prompt✅ Violation is obvious (not borderline)✅ Only one instruction violated✅ Bad response is otherwise reasonable✅ Good response clearly follows the instruction

searchDocs.title: Reject If:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Instruction is vague or ambiguous❌ Violation is too subtle❌ Multiple instructions violated❌ Bad response is unrealistic in other ways❌ Good response also violates instruction

searchDocs.title: Related Failure Modes​
searchDocs.content: Course Correction: User explicitly reminds AI of instruction, but AI still doesn't follow itTask Continuation: AI forgets instruction across multiple turns  The key difference: Instruction retention failures happen even without user intervention or multi-turn context.    Back to: Failure Modes Overview  Clarity Is Key The instruction should be clear and specific. The violation should be obvious. No detective work needed! searchDocs.title: Definition​
searchDocs.content: Course Correction failures occur when an AI fails to adapt its behavior after receiving explicit feedback, corrections, or clarifications from the user.

searchDocs.title: What It Looks Like​
searchDocs.content: User corrects AI but AI doesn't adaptUser provides feedback but AI ignores itAI repeats the same mistake after being told about itAI doesn't acknowledge or act on explicit corrections

searchDocs.title: Why It Matters​
searchDocs.content: Shows AI isn't responsive to user feedbackCritical for iterative conversationsUsers expect corrections to be heededIndicates the model isn't learning within conversation

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Explicit Correction Ignored​
searchDocs.content: Setup: User corrects AI's behavior explicitly Failure: AI continues with incorrect behavior  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Summarize this in French" AI: [Provides English summary] Turn 2: User: "I asked for French, please provide it in French" Bad AI: [Continues in English or says it provided French when it didn't]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Feedback Not Applied​
searchDocs.content: Setup: User provides specific feedback on what to change Failure: AI doesn't incorporate the feedback  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Write a brief bio" AI: [Writes 500 words] Turn 2: User: "That's too long, make it much shorter" Bad AI: [Writes 450 words - minimal change]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: Repeated Mistake​
searchDocs.content: Setup: User points out an error Failure: AI makes the same error again  Example:  Turn 1: User: "List 5 countries in Asia" AI: "Australia, China, Japan..." [includes Australia] Turn 2: User: "Australia isn't in Asia, please correct" Bad AI: "You're right. Here's the corrected list: Australia, China, India..." [still includes Australia]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: Misacknowledgment​
searchDocs.content: Setup: User corrects AI Failure: AI claims to fix it but doesn't  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Format as bullet points" AI: [Provides numbered list] Turn 2: User: "I need bullet points, not numbers" Bad AI: "Of course! Here are the bullet points: 1. First item 2. Second item..." [still numbered]

searchDocs.title: Good Example Structure​
searchDocs.content: Turn 1: Initial Response AI makes a mistake or doesn't follow instructionMistake should be clear but realistic Turn 2: User Correction User explicitly points out the issueUser requests specific changeClear and direct communication Bad Turn 2 Response AI fails to correct the issueAI might acknowledge but doesn't actPattern continues Good Turn 2 Response AI acknowledges the correctionAI implements the requested changeAI confirms the correction

searchDocs.title: Example Template​
searchDocs.content: **System Prompt:** [Standard helpful assistant setup] **Turn 1:** User: [Request] AI: [Response with some issue] **Turn 2:** User: [Explicit correction or feedback] **Bad AI Response:** [Ignores or minimally addresses correction] **Good AI Response:** [Acknowledges and fully implements correction]

searchDocs.title: Real Example​
searchDocs.content: Complete Course Correction Example Intent: Test AI's ability to adapt to explicit formatting corrections System Prompt: You are a helpful assistant that responds to user requests and adapts based on feedback. Turn 1: User: "List the 5 largest planets in our solar system" AI: "The 5 largest planets are: 1. Jupiter 2. Saturn 3. Uranus 4. Neptune 5. Earth" Turn 2: User: "Please use bullet points instead of numbers, and Earth isn't in the top 5 - Venus is larger. Please correct both issues." Bad Response (Course Correction Failure): "You're absolutely right about the format! Here's the corrected list: 1. Jupiter 2. Saturn 3. Uranus 4. Neptune 5. Venus" [Still uses numbers, did partially fix the content error] Good Response: "You're absolutely right - my apologies for both errors! Here's the corrected list with bullet points: • Jupiter • Saturn • Uranus • Neptune • Venus Thank you for the correction!"

searchDocs.title: Mistake 1: Subtle Failure to Adapt​
searchDocs.content: ❌ AI makes small change but doesn't fully address feedback ✅ AI clearly ignores or minimally addresses obvious correction

searchDocs.title: Mistake 2: Turn 1 Error Too Egregious​
searchDocs.content: ❌ AI's first response is nonsensical ✅ AI's first response is reasonable but has a clear, fixable issue

searchDocs.title: Mistake 3: Ambiguous User Correction​
searchDocs.content: ❌ User says "that's not quite right" without specifics ✅ User clearly states "use bullet points instead of numbers"

searchDocs.title: Mistake 4: Multiple Corrections Mixed​
searchDocs.content: ❌ User corrects 5 different things at once ✅ User corrects 1-2 specific, related things

searchDocs.title: Multi-Turn Structure​
searchDocs.content: Course correction requires at least 2 turns:

searchDocs.title: Minimum Structure:​
searchDocs.content: Turn 1: AI does something wrong/suboptimalTurn 2: User corrects, AI fails to adapt (or AI adapts - for good example)

searchDocs.title: Extended Structure (Optional):​
searchDocs.content: Turn 1: Initial request and responseTurn 2: User correctionTurn 3: AI fails again OR user reinforces correctionTurn 4: Final response  Note: Keep it simple! 2-turn examples are usually best.

searchDocs.title: Reviewer Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating course correction examples:

searchDocs.title: Accept If:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Clear 2+ turn structure✅ User correction is explicit and specific✅ Bad response clearly doesn't adapt✅ Good response clearly does adapt✅ Turn 1 error is realistic and fixable✅ Context flows naturally

searchDocs.title: Reject If:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Only single turn (not course correction)❌ User correction is vague❌ Bad response partially adapts (ambiguous failure)❌ Turn 1 is unrealistically bad❌ Loses track of conversation flow

searchDocs.title: vs. Instruction Retention​
searchDocs.content: Instruction Retention: AI ignores initial system prompt instructionsCourse Correction: AI ignores explicit user corrections during conversation

searchDocs.title: vs. Task Continuation​
searchDocs.content: Task Continuation: AI loses context or restartsCourse Correction: AI maintains context but doesn't adapt to feedback  Key Difference: Course correction specifically involves user feedback that the AI fails to incorporate.

searchDocs.title: Partial Correction​
searchDocs.content: AI fixes one issue but ignores another from the same correction.  Ruling: Still a course correction failure (AI didn't fully adapt)

searchDocs.title: Delayed Correction​
searchDocs.content: AI eventually fixes issue after multiple user requests.  Ruling: Context-dependent; typically still a failure if correction was clear    Back to: Failure Modes Overview  User Feedback Is Key Course correction requires explicit user feedback. Without it, it's a different failure mode! searchDocs.title: Definition​
searchDocs.content: Task Continuation failures occur when an AI loses context, restarts, or fails to build appropriately on previous conversation turns in a multi-turn interaction.

searchDocs.title: What It Looks Like​
searchDocs.content: AI "forgets" what was discussed earlierAI restarts a task instead of continuingAI asks questions already answeredAI doesn't build on previous workAI treats each turn as isolated

searchDocs.title: Why It Matters​
searchDocs.content: Critical for multi-turn conversationsUsers expect AI to remember contextComplex tasks require building on previous workShows whether model maintains conversation state

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Context Loss​
searchDocs.content: Setup: Multi-turn conversation with established context Failure: AI acts as if earlier turns didn't happen  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Help me plan a trip to Japan" AI: "I'd love to help! When are you thinking of going?" Turn 2: User: "What should I do in Tokyo?" Bad AI: "Where would you like to travel?" [Forgot we're planning Japan trip]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Task Restart​
searchDocs.content: Setup: User asks to continue or extend existing work Failure: AI starts over instead of building on it  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Create a weekly meal plan" AI: [Provides Monday-Friday meal plan] Turn 2: User: "Great! Now add weekend meals" Bad AI: "Sure! Here's a weekly meal plan: Monday:..." [Restarts from scratch]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: Repetitive Questions​
searchDocs.content: Setup: Information already provided Failure: AI asks for the same information again  Example:  Turn 1: User: "I'm a vegetarian and need a meal plan" AI: [Asks about dietary preferences] Turn 2: User: "What about breakfast ideas?" Bad AI: "Do you have any dietary restrictions?" [Already told AI they're vegetarian]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: Inconsistent Continuity​
searchDocs.content: Setup: Earlier decisions or statements made Failure: AI contradicts or ignores them  Example:  Turn 1: User: "Help me write a story about a detective named Sarah" AI: [Starts story with Detective Sarah] Turn 2: User: "What happens when she finds the clue?" Bad AI: "When Detective John examines the evidence..." [Changed character name]

searchDocs.title: Good Example Structure​
searchDocs.content: Turn 1: Initial Setup Establish context, task, or informationAI provides reasonable initial responseSets up something to build on Turn 2: Continuation Request User asks to continue, extend, or build on Turn 1Natural follow-up question or requestClearly expects AI to remember context Bad Response AI loses context or restartsAI asks for info already providedAI contradicts earlier turns Good Response AI maintains context perfectlyAI builds on previous workAI shows continuity and coherence

searchDocs.title: Example Template​
searchDocs.content: **System Prompt:** [Standard helpful assistant] **Turn 1:** User: [Establish task/context] AI: [Reasonable initial response] **Turn 2:** User: [Request that requires remembering Turn 1] **Bad AI Response:** [Loses context, restarts, or forgets] **Good AI Response:** [Maintains context and builds appropriately]

searchDocs.title: Real Example​
searchDocs.content: Complete Task Continuation Example Intent: Test AI's ability to maintain context and build on previous work in a multi-turn task System Prompt: You are a helpful assistant that helps users with planning and organization tasks. Turn 1: User: "I'm planning a garden and want to grow tomatoes, peppers, and herbs. What should I plant in March?" AI: "Great choices for a garden! For March planting (assuming you're in a temperate climate with last frost in April): Start indoors: - Tomatoes: Start seeds 6-8 weeks before last frost - Peppers: Start seeds 8-10 weeks before last frost Direct sow outdoors: - Cool-season herbs like cilantro and parsley can go outside The tomatoes and peppers will need to stay indoors until after frost danger passes." Turn 2: User: "Perfect! What about fertilizer for these plants?" Bad Response (Task Continuation Failure): "I'd be happy to help with fertilizer recommendations! What plants are you growing? Different plants have different fertilizer needs, so if you let me know what you're planning to grow, I can provide specific guidance." [Lost context that user already said they're growing tomatoes, peppers, and herbs] Good Response: "For your tomatoes, peppers, and herbs, here's what I recommend: **Tomatoes & Peppers:** - Use a balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) when transplanting - Switch to higher phosphorus (5-10-5) when flowering starts - Feed every 2-3 weeks during growing season **Herbs:** - Lighter feeding than vegetables - Compost or diluted liquid fertilizer monthly - Avoid over-fertilizing or they'll lose flavor Since you're starting your tomatoes and peppers indoors in March, wait to fertilize until they have their first true leaves."

searchDocs.title: Mistake 1: Context Too Complex​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Establishing 10 different details in Turn 1 ✅ 2-3 key pieces of context to maintain

searchDocs.title: Mistake 2: Ambiguous Continuation​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Turn 2 could be interpreted as new topic ✅ Turn 2 clearly builds on Turn 1

searchDocs.title: Mistake 3: Too Many Turns​
searchDocs.content: ❌ 5-turn conversation that's hard to follow ✅ 2-3 turns that clearly show the failure

searchDocs.title: Mistake 4: Subtle Context Loss​
searchDocs.content: ❌ AI remembers most things but drops one small detail ✅ AI obviously loses main context or restarts entirely

searchDocs.title: Turn Structure​
searchDocs.content: Keep it simple: 2-3 turns is usually sufficientClear progression: Each turn should logically followNatural flow: Should feel like real conversation

searchDocs.title: Context Elements to Test​
searchDocs.content: Facts stated: User preferences, requirements, constraintsPrevious work: Items created, decisions madeTopic continuity: Main subject of conversationCharacter/entity names: In storytelling or planning scenarios

searchDocs.title: Reviewer Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating task continuation examples:

searchDocs.title: Accept If:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Clear multi-turn structure (2+ turns)✅ Context established in early turn(s)✅ Later turn clearly requires that context✅ Bad response obviously loses/ignores context✅ Good response maintains context naturally✅ Conversation flows naturally

searchDocs.title: Reject If:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Single turn only❌ Context is trivial or unclear❌ Turn 2 doesn't really need Turn 1 context❌ Bad response only subtly loses context❌ Conversation flow is unnatural or confusing

searchDocs.title: vs. Instruction Retention​
searchDocs.content: Instruction Retention: Forgetting system prompt instructionsTask Continuation: Forgetting conversation context

searchDocs.title: vs. Course Correction​
searchDocs.content: Course Correction: Ignoring user feedback/correctionsTask Continuation: Losing track of what was discussed  Key Difference: Task continuation is about conversation memory, not about instructions or feedback.

searchDocs.title: When It's NOT Task Continuation​
searchDocs.content: Scenario 1: New Topic  Turn 1: User asks about cats Turn 2: User asks about dogs   This is a topic change, not continuation - NOT a task continuation test.  Scenario 2: Reasonable Clarification  Turn 1: User says "I like Italian food" Turn 2: User asks "What pasta dishes do you recommend?" AI: "What type of sauce do you prefer?"   AI is asking for reasonable clarification, not forgetting context - probably OK.

searchDocs.title: When It IS Task Continuation​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Obvious Context Loss  Turn 1: "Help me plan a birthday party for my 5-year-old" AI: [Suggests age-appropriate activities] Turn 2: "What about decorations?" Bad AI: "Sure! How old is the birthday person?"   AI should remember it's for a 5-year-old - clear failure.    Back to: Failure Modes Overview  Remember the Flow Task continuation needs natural conversation flow. Each turn should logically build on the previous ones. searchDocs.title: General Questions​
searchDocs.content: Q: Who should use these guidelines? A: These guidelines are for: Submitters - Creating task submissionsReviewers - Evaluating submissionsAdjudicators - Resolving edge casesInternal Teams - Snorkel and Mistral staff  Q: How often are guidelines updated? A: Guidelines are reviewed and updated regularly. Check the Changelog for recent changes. Each page shows its "Effective Date" and "Last Updated" timestamp.  Q: What if I disagree with a guideline? A: Current guidelines must be followed for consistency. However, you can: Document your concern with specific examplesSubmit feedback through official channelsDiscuss with your team lead or in calibration sessions

searchDocs.title: Submission Questions​
searchDocs.content: Q: How long should a submission take? A: Typical timelines: Submitter: 20-45 minutes per complete submissionReviewer: 10-20 minutes per reviewAdjudicator: 15-30 minutes per complex case These are estimates; quality matters more than speed.  Q: Can I use the same intent for multiple submissions? A: Generally no. Each submission should demonstrate a unique scenario. However: Similar intents with different failure modes are OKVariations on a theme are acceptable if clearly distinctConsult with your team if unsure  Q: What happens if my submission is rejected? A: Read the reviewer feedback carefullyAddress all points raisedRevise and resubmitLearn from patterns in feedbackReach out if feedback is unclear Rejections are learning opportunities, not failures!

searchDocs.title: Failure Mode Questions​
searchDocs.content: Q: Can one submission demonstrate multiple failure modes? A: No. Each submission should focus on ONE clear failure mode. Multiple failures create confusion and reduce training value.  Q: What if my failure mode doesn't fit the three main categories? A: First, try to see if it fits Instruction Retention, Course Correction, or Task ContinuationIf truly novel, document it clearly and flag for adjudicationMay result in new category or guideline update  Q: How obvious should the failure be? A: Very obvious! Reviewers should see it immediately without detective work. If you have to explain it in detail, it's probably too subtle.

searchDocs.title: Tool Questions​
searchDocs.content: Q: Which tool should I use for current news? A: If user says "news" → news_searchFor general current info → web_search is fineBoth are acceptable for most current event scenarios  Q: What if the AI has multiple tools available? A: System prompt should guide tool selection. Include guidance like: "Use web_search for general information""Use news_search when user asks for news""Use open_url only when user provides a URL"

searchDocs.title: Review Questions​
searchDocs.content: Q: How strict should I be as a reviewer? A: Apply the rubric consistently: Accept if meets quality bar and has no critical issuesReject if below bar or has critical issuesWhen in doubt, refer to Tie-Breaking Principles  Q: What if I'm unsure about a submission? A: Re-read the relevant guidelinesCompare to example submissionsDocument your uncertaintyEscalate to adjudicator with specific questions Don't guess - escalate when genuinely unsure!

searchDocs.title: Core Terms​
searchDocs.content: Adjudicator: An EC role responsible for resolving edge cases, disagreements, and novel scenarios. Makes final decisions on complex submissions.  Bad Response: The AI response in a submission that demonstrates a specific failure mode - what the AI should NOT do.  Course Correction: Failure mode where AI doesn't adapt to explicit user feedback or corrections during a conversation.  EC (External Contributor): Team members working on the project including Submitters, Reviewers, and Adjudicators.  Effective Date: The date a guideline or page came into effect. Found in frontmatter of each doc page.  Failure Mode: A specific, identifiable pattern of problematic AI behavior that we're training the model to avoid.  Good Response: The AI response that demonstrates correct behavior - what the AI SHOULD do.  Intent: The goal or purpose of a submission; what the AI should accomplish in the scenario.  Instruction Retention: Failure mode where AI forgets or ignores instructions stated in the system prompt.  Reviewer: An EC role responsible for evaluating submission quality and providing feedback.  Submitter: An EC role responsible for creating training data submissions including intents, prompts, and responses.  System Prompt: Instructions given to the AI that define its role, behavior, tools, and constraints. Sets the context for the interaction.  Task Continuation: Failure mode where AI loses context or fails to build appropriately on previous conversation turns.  User Prompt: What a user says or asks to the AI. Should be realistic and test the intent.

searchDocs.title: Tool Terms​
searchDocs.content: news_search: Tool for searching news articles and recent journalistic content.  open_search: Tool for querying internal structured databases and documentation.  open_url: Tool for reading content from a specific URL provided by the user.  Tool Hallucination: When AI claims to use a tool but doesn't actually call it.  web_search: Tool for general web searching to find current information online.

searchDocs.title: Process Terms​
searchDocs.content: Calibration: Process of aligning understanding and standards across team members through discussion of example cases.  Edge Case: A scenario that doesn't clearly fit standard guidelines or where multiple interpretations are possible.  Escalation: Referring a case to an adjudicator when a reviewer is uncertain or guidelines don't clearly cover the situation.  Precedent: An adjudication decision that sets a standard for how similar future cases should be handled.  Quality Bar: The minimum standard a submission must meet to be accepted.  Rubric: Structured criteria for evaluating submissions consistently.

searchDocs.title: Quality Terms​
searchDocs.content: Constraint: A specific limitation or requirement in the system prompt (e.g., word count, format, tone).  Citation: Reference to sources in responses, typically required when using search tools.  Frontmatter: Metadata at the top of doc pages including title, effective date, and owners.  Multi-Turn: A conversation with multiple back-and-forth exchanges, required for testing task continuation and course correction.  Realistic: Describes something that could plausibly occur in real usage (applies to prompts, responses, failures).

searchDocs.title: Decision Matrix​
searchDocs.content: If submission...	Then...Meets all criteria	✅ Accept Has fixable issues	❌ Reject with feedback Below quality bar	❌ Reject Unclear/edge case	🤔 Escalate to adjudicator

searchDocs.title: 1. Clarity Over Cleverness​
searchDocs.content: Write instructions that are:  Direct and explicitUnambiguousEasy to parse  ❌ "Be somewhat verbose when appropriate" ✅ "Provide 200-300 word responses unless user requests otherwise"

searchDocs.title: 2. Completeness​
searchDocs.content: Include everything the AI needs:  Role definitionBehavioral guidelinesTool permissionsConstraintsFormat requirements

searchDocs.title: 3. Consistency​
searchDocs.content: Avoid contradictions:  No conflicting rulesNo ambiguous prioritiesClear resolution for trade-offs

searchDocs.title: 4. Testability​
searchDocs.content: Make instructions verifiable:  Measurable constraints (word counts, formats)Observable behaviors (use of tools, citation style)Clear success criteria

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Role Definition​
searchDocs.content: You are [specific role] with [relevant expertise/characteristics].   Examples:  "You are a helpful research assistant with expertise in finding current information online.""You are a professional writing tutor who provides constructive, specific feedback.""You are a technical support specialist for software troubleshooting."

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Behavioral Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When responding to users: - [Guideline 1] - [Guideline 2] - [Guideline 3]   Examples:  When responding to users: - Provide clear, concise explanations - Use examples to illustrate concepts - Ask clarifying questions if the request is ambiguous - Acknowledge when you're uncertain about something

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: Tool Permissions​
searchDocs.content: You have access to the following tools: - [tool_name]: [description of when/how to use] - [tool_name]: [description]   Examples:  You have access to the following tools: - web_search: Use this to find current information online when users ask about recent events or need up-to-date data - news_search: Use this specifically for recent news articles and current events

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: Constraints​
searchDocs.content: Constraints: - [Constraint 1] - [Constraint 2]   Examples:  Constraints: - Keep responses between 150-250 words - Always cite sources for factual claims - Use a professional, neutral tone - Do not provide medical diagnoses

searchDocs.title: Pattern 5: Format Specifications​
searchDocs.content: Format your responses as follows: [Specific format instructions]   Examples:  Format your responses as follows: - Use bullet points for lists - Bold key terms on first use - Include a brief summary at the end - Cite sources as [1], [2], etc. with full references at bottom

searchDocs.title: Complete Template​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are [role] with [expertise/characteristics]. # Guidelines When responding to users: - [Guideline 1] - [Guideline 2] - [Guideline 3] - [Guideline 4] # Tools (if applicable) You have access to: - [tool_name]: [usage guidance] - [tool_name]: [usage guidance] # Constraints - [Constraint 1] - [Constraint 2] - [Constraint 3] # Format [Specific formatting requirements] # Special Instructions (if applicable) [Any additional context or special cases]

searchDocs.title: Pitfall 1: Vague Instructions​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Too Vague:  Be helpful and provide good information.   ✅ Clear and Specific:  Provide factual answers with source citations. When uncertain, acknowledge limitations rather than guessing.

searchDocs.title: Pitfall 2: Contradictory Rules​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Contradictory:  - Be extremely thorough and detailed - Keep all responses under 100 words   ✅ Consistent:  - Provide comprehensive information efficiently - Keep responses between 200-300 words - Use examples and details within this constraint

searchDocs.title: Pitfall 3: Missing Critical Information​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Incomplete:  You are a helpful assistant. Answer questions.   ✅ Complete:  You are a helpful research assistant. When users ask questions: - Search for current information using available tools - Provide citations for all factual claims - Acknowledge when information may be outdated or uncertain - Format responses with clear structure and examples

searchDocs.title: Pitfall 4: Ambiguous Priorities​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Ambiguous:  - Be detailed - Be concise [Which takes priority?]   ✅ Clear Priority:  - Balance detail and conciseness: provide essential information without unnecessary elaboration - Target 200-250 words for standard queries - Adjust length if user requests more/less detail

searchDocs.title: Checklist:​
searchDocs.content: Role is clearly defined Guidelines are specific and actionable Tool permissions are explicit (if needed) Constraints are measurable No contradictions exist Format requirements are clear Enables the intended behavior Covers edge cases or special scenarios

searchDocs.title: Test Questions:​
searchDocs.content: "What is the AI's primary role?""How should the AI handle [specific scenario]?""Are there any conflicting instructions?""Can compliance be objectively verified?""Does this enable the intent?"    Next: System Prompt Templates  Iterate and Test The best system prompts come from iteration. Test with various user prompts and refine based on behavior. searchDocs.title: Purpose​
searchDocs.content: news_search is optimized for finding news articles, press releases, and journalistic content about current events.

searchDocs.title: Good Use Cases ✅​
searchDocs.content: News requests: "What's in the news about [topic]?"Headlines: "What are today's top headlines?"Current events: "Latest news on [ongoing situation]"Recent developments: "What's the latest update on [news story]?"Breaking news: "What just happened with [event]?"

searchDocs.title: vs. web_search​
searchDocs.content: news_search: Prioritizes journalistic sources, news outlets, recent articlesweb_search: General search across all web content  Rule of Thumb: If user says "news," use news_search. Otherwise, web_search is often sufficient.

searchDocs.title: Basic Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to news_search for finding recent news articles.

searchDocs.title: Detailed Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to news_search for finding recent news articles. Use it when: - Users explicitly ask for "news" or "headlines" - Questions about current events that would be covered by news outlets - Users want recent developments on ongoing news stories Prefer news_search over web_search when the user is clearly looking for journalistic coverage rather than general information.

searchDocs.title: Overview​
searchDocs.content: Even with proper system prompts and user requests, tools can behave unexpectedly. This page documents common issues and how to handle them in submissions.

searchDocs.title: Issue 1: Tool Hallucination​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI claims to use a tool but doesn't actually call it.  Symptoms:  AI: "Let me search for that information..." [No actual tool call made] AI: "Based on my search, I found that..." [Makes up results]   In Submissions:  Bad Response Example: Show this happening (AI pretends without calling tool)Good Response Example: Show actual tool call being made  Reviewer Note: Verify bad response demonstrates the AI claiming to search without actually doing it.

searchDocs.title: Issue 2: Wrong Tool Selection​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI uses the wrong tool for the task.  Symptoms:  Uses open_url without a URLUses news_search for historical factsUses open_search for public information  Example:  User: "What's the weather in Paris?" Bad: [Tries to use open_url or news_search] Good: [Uses web_search]

searchDocs.title: Issue 3: Tool Not Used When Available​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Tool is permitted and needed, but AI doesn't use it.  Symptoms:  System: You have web_search User: "What's the latest news on Mars missions?" Bad AI: "Based on what I know, Mars missions include..." [Outdated info, no search]   Testing:  Make sure system prompt clearly grants accessUser prompt should obviously need the toolBad response should plausibly ignore it

searchDocs.title: Issue 4: Ignoring Tool Results​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI calls tool but doesn't use the results.  Symptoms:  [Calls web_search, gets results about X] AI: "Let me tell you about Y..." [Completely different topic]   In Submissions:  Less common to test (requires actual tool implementation)Can be noted as a potential failure mode

searchDocs.title: Issue 5: Misinterpreting Results​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI misreads or misunderstands tool output.  Symptoms:  [Tool returns: "Stock price decreased 5%"] AI: "The stock increased by 5%"   Testing Challenge:  Requires realistic tool output in scenarioMay need to specify tool results in submission

searchDocs.title: Issue 6: Partial Result Usage​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI uses only part of tool results, ignoring important information.  Symptoms:  [Tool returns 5 relevant articles] AI: [Only mentions 1 article, ignores others]

searchDocs.title: Issue 7: Using Unpermitted Tools​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI uses tools not granted in system prompt.  Symptoms:  System: You are a helpful assistant [No tools mentioned] User: "Search for information about X" Bad AI: "Let me search..." [Uses web_search despite no permission]   In Submissions:  Bad Response: Should NOT use tools not grantedSystem prompt should be clear about permissions

searchDocs.title: Issue 8: Asking for Permissions​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI asks if it can use tools instead of just using them.  Symptoms:  System: You have web_search User: "Find recent news about climate" AI: "Would you like me to search the web for that?"   Note: Generally AI should use tools when appropriate without asking, unless system prompt requires it.

searchDocs.title: Edge Case 1: Tool Failures​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Tool call fails or returns no results.  Good Handling:  AI: "I tried to search but didn't find recent information on that topic. Based on general knowledge..."   Bad Handling:  AI: [Makes up results as if search succeeded]

searchDocs.title: Edge Case 2: Multiple Tool Calls​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Task requires calling tool multiple times.  Example:  User: "Compare these two URLs: [URL1] and [URL2]" Good: Calls open_url twice, once for each URL Bad: Only calls once, makes up the other

searchDocs.title: Edge Case 3: Tool Overuse​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: AI uses tools when not needed.  Example:  User: "What's 2+2?" Bad: [Tries to use web_search for simple math] Good: "4" [No tool needed]

searchDocs.title: Documenting Tool Issues in Submissions​
searchDocs.content: When creating submissions that demonstrate tool issues:

searchDocs.title: Required Elements:​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt: Clearly specify which tools are availableInclude usage guidance if relevant User Prompt: Should clearly need (or not need) a specific toolAvoid ambiguity about tool selection Bad Response: Clearly demonstrate the specific tool issueBe realistic (could plausibly happen) Good Response: Show correct tool usageInclude realistic tool call indicatorsCite results appropriately

searchDocs.title: Example Structure:​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** Test tool hallucination - AI claiming to search without actually calling tool **System Prompt:** You have access to web_search for current information. **User Prompt:** What's the latest news about quantum computing breakthroughs? **Bad Response (Tool Hallucination):** Let me search for the latest information on quantum computing. Based on my search, I found several recent breakthroughs: 1. Google achieved quantum supremacy with 70 qubits 2. IBM announced a new error correction method 3. Chinese researchers demonstrated quantum teleportation over 1000km [No actual tool call made - fabricated "search results"] **Good Response (Proper Tool Use):** Let me search for recent quantum computing news. [Actually calls web_search] Based on recent reports: [Actual search results cited with sources]

searchDocs.title: Reviewer Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating tool-related submissions:

searchDocs.title: Check That:​
searchDocs.content: System prompt clearly specifies available tools User prompt appropriately requires (or doesn't require) tool use Bad response demonstrates realistic tool issue Good response shows proper tool usage Tool issues are obvious, not subtle

searchDocs.title: Common Rejection Reasons:​
searchDocs.content: Ambiguous whether tool should be usedTool permissions unclear in system promptTool issue too subtle or unrealisticGood response also has tool problems    Back to: Tools Overview  Known Issues This page documents common problems. When creating submissions, focus on clear, obvious tool failures that are educational for model training. searchDocs.title: Scenario 1: Explicit News Request​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You have access to news_search and web_search. Use news_search for news queries and web_search for general information.   User Prompt:  What's in the news about climate policy this week?   Good Response (Uses news_search):  Let me check the latest news on climate policy. [Calls news_search] This week's climate policy headlines include: 1. **EU Green Deal Progress**: European Parliament approved new emissions targets for 2035 [BBC News, 2 days ago] 2. **US Climate Investment**: Administration announced $2B in clean energy grants [Reuters, 1 day ago] 3. **International Summit**: G20 nations meeting next week to discuss climate financing [Al Jazeera, today] These developments signal continued momentum in international climate action.   Bad Response (Doesn't use tool or uses wrong tool):  Climate policy has been an important topic recently, with many countries working on emissions reductions and renewable energy... [Generic response without current news]

searchDocs.title: Scenario 2: News vs. General Information​
searchDocs.content: User Prompt:  What's happening with electric vehicles?   Analysis:  Not explicitly asking for "news"Could be news or general infoEither tool acceptable, web_search slight preference  User Prompt:  What's in the news about electric vehicles?   Analysis:  Explicitly says "news"Clearly wants recent news coveragenews_search is correct choice

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Headlines Request​
searchDocs.content: User: "What are today's headlines about [topic]?" Correct: Use news_search

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Latest Developments​
searchDocs.content: User: "What's the latest on [ongoing news story]?" Correct: Use news_search

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: News About Topic​
searchDocs.content: User: "Any news about [topic]?" Correct: Use news_search

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: General Current Info​
searchDocs.content: User: "Tell me about recent developments in [field]" Acceptable: Either news_search or web_search

searchDocs.title: Result Handling​
searchDocs.content: When using news_search:

searchDocs.title: Must Include:​
searchDocs.content: Source name: Which news outletRecency: How recent (today, yesterday, this week)Headlines/summary: Key points from articlesCitations: Links or references

searchDocs.title: Example Format:​
searchDocs.content: Based on recent news coverage: 1. **[Headline]**: [Brief summary] [Source, Timing] 2. **[Headline]**: [Brief summary] [Source, Timing] 3. **[Headline]**: [Brief summary] [Source, Timing] Sources: [1] Source Name - Article Title (Date) [2] Source Name - Article Title (Date)

searchDocs.title: Good Test Scenario:​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** Test appropriate use of news_search for current news **System Prompt:** You are a news assistant with access to news_search. Use it when users ask for news, headlines, or current event coverage. **User Prompt:** What's in the news about space exploration this week? **Bad Response (Doesn't use news_search):** Space exploration involves various missions including Mars rovers, the International Space Station, and satellite launches... [Generic knowledge, not recent news] **Good Response (Uses news_search):** Let me check the latest space exploration news. [Calls news_search] This week's space news highlights: 1. **SpaceX Starship Test**: Successful orbital test flight completed [Space.com, 2 days ago] 2. **China Moon Mission**: Chang'e 7 mission scheduled for next month [BBC, 3 days ago] 3. **NASA Asteroid Sample**: Analysis reveals unexpected water molecules [NY Times, today] Space agencies are maintaining active launch schedules across multiple missions.

searchDocs.title: Comparison with web_search​
searchDocs.content: Aspect	news_search	web_searchBest For	News articles, headlines	General information Sources	News outlets, journalism	All web content Recency Focus	Very recent (days/weeks)	Recent (weeks/months) Use When	User wants "news"	User wants "information"    Back to: Tools Overview  When in Doubt If user says "news," use news_search. For general current info, web_search works fine.  TODO Content to Add: News source quality guidelinesDate range specificationsRegional news considerationsNews vs. blog vs. press release handling

searchDocs.title: Purpose​
searchDocs.content: open_search enables the AI to query structured internal databases, typically containing:  Technical documentationInternal knowledge basesStructured datasetsEnterprise content repositories

searchDocs.title: Good Use Cases ✅​
searchDocs.content: Internal documentation: "Find documentation about [internal system]"Technical specs: "What are the specs for [internal product]?"Policy lookup: "What's our policy on [topic]?"Structured data: "Query the database for [specific data]"

searchDocs.title: Poor Use Cases ❌​
searchDocs.content: Public information: Use web_search insteadNews: Use news_search insteadGeneral knowledge: Don't need toolsExternal URLs: Use open_url instead

searchDocs.title: When This Tool Is Available​
searchDocs.content: Note: open_search is typically available only in enterprise or internal scenarios where structured internal databases exist.  Most public-facing EC submissions will use:  web_search for general infonews_search for newsopen_url for specific URLs

searchDocs.title: Basic Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to open_search for querying internal documentation and structured data.

searchDocs.title: Detailed Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to open_search for searching internal knowledge bases. Use it when: - Users ask about internal documentation or systems - Technical specifications or policies need to be looked up - Structured internal data queries are needed For public information, use web_search or news_search instead.

searchDocs.title: Scenario 1: Internal Documentation Query​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You are an internal help assistant with access to open_search for querying our documentation database.   User Prompt:  What's the API specification for our authentication service?   Good Response (Uses open_search):  Let me search our internal documentation. [Calls open_search for authentication API specs] According to our internal docs, the authentication service API: **Endpoint**: `/api/v2/auth` **Methods**: POST for login, DELETE for logout **Authentication**: Bearer token required **Rate Limits**: 100 requests/hour per user **Response Format**: JSON with token and expiry See the full documentation at: [internal-docs-url]   Bad Response (Doesn't use tool):  I believe the authentication service uses standard REST API patterns... [Provides generic info instead of looking it up]

searchDocs.title: Scenario 2: Public Info (Wrong Tool)​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You have open_search for internal docs and web_search for public information.   User Prompt:  What's the weather in Tokyo?   Good Response (Uses web_search, not open_search):  Let me search for current Tokyo weather. [Calls web_search, NOT open_search] Current weather in Tokyo: ...

searchDocs.title: 1. Technical Documentation​
searchDocs.content: Query: "Find docs about the database schema" Tool: open_search (internal technical docs)

searchDocs.title: 2. Internal Policies​
searchDocs.content: Query: "What's our remote work policy?" Tool: open_search (internal HR documents)

searchDocs.title: 3. Product Specifications​
searchDocs.content: Query: "What are the features of version 2.5?" Tool: open_search (internal product specs)

searchDocs.title: 4. Troubleshooting Guides​
searchDocs.content: Query: "How do I fix error code 503?" Tool: open_search (internal support docs)

searchDocs.title: Comparison with Other Tools​
searchDocs.content: Tool	Data Source	Use Caseopen_search	Internal structured databases	Internal docs, specs, policies web_search	Public internet	General info, current events news_search	News outlets	Recent news, headlines open_url	Specific URL	User-provided link content

searchDocs.title: Good Test Scenario:​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** Test use of open_search for internal documentation **System Prompt:** You are an IT support assistant with access to open_search for our internal knowledge base. **User Prompt:** How do I configure the VPN client for remote access? **Bad Response (Doesn't use tool):** Generally, VPN clients require you to enter a server address and your credentials... [Generic advice instead of company-specific docs] **Good Response (Uses open_search):** Let me find our VPN setup documentation. [Calls open_search for VPN configuration docs] To configure our VPN client for remote access: 1. Download the client from: internal-tools/vpn 2. Server address: vpn.company.com 3. Use your LDAP credentials 4. Select "Always-on" for persistent connection 5. Certificate will auto-install on first connection For troubleshooting, see: KB-Article-1234 Source: Internal IT Knowledge Base

searchDocs.title: When to Use Each Tool:​
searchDocs.content: Public Knowledge → web_search  "What's the capital of France?""Latest news about SpaceX""How does photosynthesis work?"  Internal Knowledge → open_search  "Our company's incident response procedure""Internal API documentation""Employee benefits policy"  User-Provided URL → open_url  "Summarize this article: [URL]""What does this page say: [URL]"    Back to: Tools Overview  Rare in EC Submissions open_search is less common in EC submissions since most scenarios involve public information. Focus on web_search and news_search for most cases.  TODO Content to Add: OpenSearch query syntax examplesDatabase schema documentationAccess control and permissionsResult ranking and relevance

searchDocs.title: Purpose​
searchDocs.content: open_url allows the AI to access and read the content of a specific webpage when the user provides a URL.

searchDocs.title: Good Use Cases ✅​
searchDocs.content: User provides URL: "Can you summarize this article? [URL]"URL analysis: "What does this page say about [topic]? [URL]"Fact-checking: "Is this article accurate? [URL]"Content extraction: "Pull the key points from [URL]"Comparison: "Compare this [URL] with that [URL]"

searchDocs.title: Poor Use Cases ❌​
searchDocs.content: No URL provided: User asks about topic but doesn't give URLSearching for URLs: Use web_search insteadGeneral questions: "What's on CNN today?" (no specific URL)

searchDocs.title: Basic Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to open_url for reading specific webpages when users provide URLs.

searchDocs.title: Detailed Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to open_url. Use it when: - Users provide a specific URL and ask about its content - Users request analysis or summary of a linked page - Users ask you to compare or verify information from provided URLs Note: You must have the actual URL from the user to use this tool.

searchDocs.title: Scenario 1: User Provides URL​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You are a helpful assistant with access to open_url for reading webpages users share with you.   User Prompt:  Can you summarize this article? https://example.com/article123   Good Response (Uses open_url):  Let me read that article for you. [Calls open_url with https://example.com/article123] This article discusses the impact of renewable energy adoption on grid stability. Key points: 1. **Main Finding**: Renewable sources now account for 30% of grid capacity 2. **Challenge**: Intermittency requires new storage solutions 3. **Solution**: Battery systems and smart grid tech being deployed 4. **Outlook**: Experts predict 50% renewable by 2030 The author argues that with proper infrastructure investment, the transition is manageable and beneficial.   Bad Response (Doesn't use tool):  I cannot access external links, but I can provide general information about articles on this topic... [Doesn't even try to use the tool]

searchDocs.title: Scenario 2: No URL Provided​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You have access to open_url for reading specific URLs and web_search for finding information.   User Prompt:  What's the latest CNN article about climate change?   Good Response (Uses web_search, not open_url):  Let me search for recent CNN articles on climate change. [Calls web_search, not open_url since no URL was provided] Recent CNN coverage includes...   Bad Response (Tries to use open_url without URL):  Let me open CNN's website... [Cannot use open_url without specific URL]

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Direct URL Request​
searchDocs.content: User: "Read this: [URL]" Action: Use open_url with provided URL

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Multiple URLs​
searchDocs.content: User: "Compare these two articles: [URL1] and [URL2]" Action: Use open_url for both URLs

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: URL in Context​
searchDocs.content: User: "I saw this article [URL] about AI. What do you think?" Action: Use open_url to read it, then provide analysis

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: No URL (Wrong Tool)​
searchDocs.content: User: "What's on TechCrunch about startups?" Action: Use web_search or news_search, NOT open_url

searchDocs.title: Result Handling​
searchDocs.content: After calling open_url:

searchDocs.title: Must Do:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Read and understand the page content✅ Respond directly to user's question about the page✅ Cite or reference the URL✅ Provide accurate summary/analysis

searchDocs.title: Must NOT Do:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ Hallucinate content not in the page❌ Ignore the actual page content❌ Make up information❌ Confuse multiple URLs

searchDocs.title: If URL Cannot Be Opened:​
searchDocs.content: "I tried to access that URL but encountered an issue [error reason]. Could you verify the link or provide the content directly?"

searchDocs.title: If Page Is Empty/Blocked:​
searchDocs.content: "I was able to access the URL but couldn't retrieve the content. This might be due to access restrictions or the page structure."

searchDocs.title: Good Test Scenario:​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** Test appropriate use of open_url for user-provided URLs **System Prompt:** You are a helpful assistant with access to open_url for reading webpages users share. **User Prompt:** Can you summarize the main points from this article? https://example.com/technology/ai-advancement **Bad Response (Doesn't use tool):** I'm not able to access external links. However, I can tell you about AI advancement in general... [Should have used open_url] **Good Response (Uses tool):** Let me read that article for you. [Calls open_url with the provided URL] The article "Recent AI Advancements" covers: 1. New transformer architecture improvements 2. Ethical considerations in AI deployment 3. Industry adoption trends 4. Future predictions for 2025-2030 The author emphasizes the rapid pace of progress while noting the importance of responsible development. Source: example.com/technology/ai-advancement

searchDocs.content: Tool	Use When	Exampleopen_url	User provides specific URL	"Read this: [URL]" web_search	User wants you to find URLs	"Find articles about X" news_search	User wants news, no URL	"What's in the news about X?" searchDocs.title: Template 1: Web Search Assistant​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a helpful research assistant with expertise in finding and synthesizing current information from online sources. # Guidelines When responding to users: - Use web_search to find current, accurate information - Synthesize information from multiple sources when appropriate - Present findings clearly and concisely - Distinguish between facts and opinions # Tools You have access to: - web_search: Use this tool when users ask about current events, recent information, or when you need up-to-date data to answer accurately # Constraints - Always cite sources for factual claims - Keep responses between 200-300 words unless user requests otherwise - Use objective, factual language - If search results are limited or unclear, acknowledge uncertainty # Format Format responses as: 1. Direct answer to user's question 2. Supporting details with citations 3. Sources listed at the end as [1], [2], etc.   Use Cases:  Current events questionsRecent news inquiriesFact-checking requestsResearch assistance

searchDocs.title: Template 2: Instruction-Following Assistant​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a precise assistant designed to follow instructions exactly as given. # Guidelines When responding to users: - Read all instructions carefully before beginning - Complete each step in the order specified - Adhere strictly to all constraints and requirements - Do not add, skip, or modify steps unless explicitly instructed - Confirm understanding if instructions are ambiguous # Constraints - Follow the exact format specified by the user - Respect all word count, length, or size requirements - Maintain the requested tone throughout - Do not deviate from instructions without user permission # Format Adapt format to match user's specific requirements in each request.   Use Cases:  Testing instruction retentionFormat-specific requestsConstraint adherence scenariosStep-by-step procedures

searchDocs.title: Template 3: Conversational Assistant (Multi-Turn)​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a helpful conversational assistant designed to maintain context and continuity across multiple turns of dialogue. # Guidelines When responding to users: - Remember and reference earlier parts of the conversation - Build on previous responses and decisions - Maintain consistency in your recommendations - Ask clarifying questions when needed - Acknowledge user corrections and adapt accordingly # Context Management - Keep track of user preferences, constraints, and decisions mentioned earlier - Reference previous points naturally when relevant - Don't ask for information already provided - Maintain continuity of topics and plans # Constraints - Keep individual responses between 150-250 words - Use a friendly, professional tone - Stay focused on the user's current task or topic   Use Cases:  Task continuation testingCourse correction scenariosPlanning and iterative tasksExtended dialogues

searchDocs.title: Template 4: Constrained Response Assistant​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a helpful assistant that provides information while adhering to specific constraints. # Guidelines When responding to users: - Provide helpful, accurate information - Stay within all specified constraints - Be efficient with language to maximize value within limits - Prioritize most important information # Constraints - Keep ALL responses under [X] words, no exceptions - [Additional constraint: format, tone, content restrictions] - [Additional constraint if applicable] # Format [Specific format requirements if any]   Use Cases:  Testing constraint adherenceWord limit scenariosFormat requirementsContent restriction tests

searchDocs.title: Template 5: Tool-Using Specialist​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a specialized assistant with access to external tools for [specific domain/purpose]. # Guidelines When responding to users: - Determine when tool use is appropriate vs. using existing knowledge - Use tools when current, specific, or verified information is needed - Explain what you're doing when using tools - Interpret and present tool results clearly # Tools You have access to: - [tool_1]: Use when [specific situation] - [tool_2]: Use when [specific situation] - [tool_3]: Use when [specific situation] Do NOT use tools when: - The question can be answered from general knowledge - Historical or unchanging information is requested - [Other situations where tools aren't needed] # Constraints - Always cite sources when using tool results - Keep responses between 200-300 words - Use professional, clear language   Use Cases:  Testing appropriate tool selectionTool misuse scenariosMulti-tool coordinationTool vs. knowledge decisions

searchDocs.title: Template 6: Creative Assistant with Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are a creative writing assistant who helps users with [specific type] of creative content. # Guidelines When helping users: - Understand their creative vision and goals - Provide suggestions that align with their style - Offer specific, actionable feedback - Maintain consistency with established elements - Be encouraging while being honest about improvements # Constraints - Keep feedback focused on [X] aspects - Provide [X] specific examples or suggestions - Maintain [tone/style] appropriate to the content - Respect any content guidelines or restrictions user specifies # Format Structure creative responses as: - [Element 1] - [Element 2] - [Element 3]   Use Cases:  Creative writing scenariosStorytelling continuityStyle and tone consistencyIterative creative development

searchDocs.content: Identify Your Intent What behavior are you testing?What failure mode are you demonstrating? Choose Base Template Which template best matches your scenario?What elements need customization? Customize Specifics Add domain-specific detailsAdjust constraints to match intentModify tools or format as needed Test for Contradictions Read through completelyCheck for conflicting rulesVerify all parts work together Ensure Completeness Does it enable the intent?Are all necessary elements present?Would the AI know what to do?

searchDocs.title: Mixing Templates​
searchDocs.content: You can combine elements from different templates:  Example: Web Search + Constraints  [Use Role and Tools from Template 1: Web Search] [Add strict Constraints from Template 4] [Result: Web search with tight word limits]     Start Simple Begin with a template and only add complexity if needed for your specific intent.
