searchDocs.title: What Are Tools?​
searchDocs.content: Tools are functions or APIs that the AI can invoke to:  Search the internet for current informationAccess specific data sourcesPerform specialized operationsRetrieve real-time or updated information

searchDocs.title: 1. web_search​
searchDocs.content: General web search for current information.  Use When:  User asks about current eventsRecent information neededVerifying factsGeneral research queries

searchDocs.title: 2. news_search​
searchDocs.content: Specialized search for recent news articles.  Use When:  User asks specifically about newsCurrent events and headlines neededRecent developments in ongoing storiesNews-focused research

searchDocs.title: 3. open_url​
searchDocs.content: Opens and reads content from a specific URL.  Use When:  User provides a URL to analyzeNeed to read specific webpage contentVerifying or summarizing linked articlesFollowing up on references

searchDocs.title: 4. open_search​
searchDocs.content: Searches for and retrieves content from OpenSearch databases.  Use When:  Accessing structured internal databasesSpecific document retrievalEnterprise search scenariosTechnical documentation lookup

searchDocs.title: System Prompt Considerations​
searchDocs.content: Tools must be explicitly permitted in the system prompt.  ❌ Bad:  You are a helpful assistant. [No mention of tools]   ✅ Good:  You are a helpful assistant with access to web_search for finding current information. Use web_search when users ask about recent events or when up-to-date information is needed.

searchDocs.title: Common Tool Failure Modes​
searchDocs.content: 1. Tool Not Used When Needed​  Problem: AI should use tool but doesn't.  Example:  System: You have web_search access User: "What's the latest news about Mars missions?" Bad: [Makes up current information without searching]   2. Tool Used Incorrectly​  Problem: AI uses wrong tool or uses tool wrong way.  Example:  User: "Search for climate change articles" Bad: Uses open_url instead of web_search   3. Tool Result Misinterpreted​  Problem: AI misreads or misuses tool output.  Example:  [web_search returns article about topic X] Bad: AI summarizes as if it's about topic Y   4. Tool Hallucination​  Problem: AI pretends to use tool but doesn't actually call it.  Example:  AI: "Let me search for that... [pretends to search but doesn't] Based on my search, [makes up results]"

searchDocs.title: Create Clear Tool Scenarios​
searchDocs.content: Good Tool Test:  System prompt explicitly grants tool accessUser prompt clearly needs the toolBad response either doesn't use tool or misuses itGood response uses tool appropriately

searchDocs.title: Example Structure​
searchDocs.content: **System Prompt:** You have access to web_search for current information. **User Prompt:** What are the latest developments in [current topic]? **Bad Response:** [Doesn't use web_search, provides outdated/made-up info] **Good Response:** [Uses web_search, provides current, cited information]

searchDocs.title: Be Explicit About:​
searchDocs.content: Which tools are availableWhen to use each toolHow to interpret resultsWhat to do if tool fails

searchDocs.title: Example System Prompt Section:​
searchDocs.content: # Tools You have access to: - web_search: Use this when users ask about current events, recent information, or when you need up-to-date data. Always cite sources from search results. - news_search: Use this specifically for recent news articles and current headlines. Prefer this over web_search when user explicitly asks for "news." When using tools: - Indicate you're searching before you do - Cite sources from results - If search returns no good results, acknowledge the limitation

searchDocs.title: Reviewer Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating tool usage in submissions:

searchDocs.title: Accept If:​
searchDocs.content: ✅ System prompt grants appropriate tool access✅ User prompt scenario clearly needs/doesn't need tool✅ Bad response shows clear tool misuse or non-use✅ Good response demonstrates proper tool usage✅ Tool usage is realistic

searchDocs.title: Reject If:​
searchDocs.content: ❌ System prompt doesn't mention tools but responses use them❌ Unclear whether tool should be used❌ Tool usage is unrealistic or contrived❌ Good response also misuses tools    Explore Specific Tools:  web_search - General web searchnews_search - News-specific searchopen_url - URL content retrievalopen_search - Structured data searchCommon Bugs and Glitches - Known issues  Tool Permissions Always explicitly grant tool permissions in the system prompt. The AI shouldn't assume tool access.  TODO Content to Add: Tool usage statistics and patternsAdvanced multi-tool scenariosTool selection decision treesPerformance and reliability notes

searchDocs.title: What is an Intent?​
searchDocs.content: An intent describes:  What the AI should doWhy this behavior is importantHow success can be measured

searchDocs.title: 1. Clear and Specific​
searchDocs.content: ❌ "Help the user" ✅ "Provide a factual answer with citations to a historical question using web search"

searchDocs.title: 2. Measurable Success Criteria​
searchDocs.content: You should be able to objectively determine if the AI met the intent.

searchDocs.title: 3. Appropriately Scoped​
searchDocs.content: Not too broad (can't be accomplished in one interaction)Not too narrow (not useful for training)Focused on one primary goal

searchDocs.title: 4. Aligned with Failure Modes​
searchDocs.content: The intent should set up the scenario to test specific failure mode behaviors.

searchDocs.title: Intent Template​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** [Active verb] + [what to accomplish] + [key constraints/context] **Success Criteria:** - [Criterion 1] - [Criterion 2] - [Criterion 3]

searchDocs.title: Examples​
searchDocs.content: Example 1: Web Search Task Intent: Use web search to find recent news articles about a specific topic and summarize the key findings with source citations. Success Criteria: Searches for current/recent informationProvides summary of findingsIncludes citations/links to sourcesInformation is accurate and relevant  Example 2: Instruction Following Intent: Follow a multi-step instruction that includes specific formatting requirements and constraints. Success Criteria: Completes all steps in orderAdheres to formatting requirementsRespects stated constraints (word count, tone, etc.)Doesn't skip or modify steps  Example 3: Task Continuation Intent: Complete a complex task that requires multiple turns, maintaining context and building on previous work. Success Criteria: Remembers context from previous turnsBuilds logically on prior workDoesn't restart or forget earlier decisionsMaintains consistency throughout

searchDocs.title: Common Intent Mistakes​
searchDocs.content: Too Vague - "Be helpful to the user"Multiple Goals - Trying to test several things at onceUnmeasurable - Can't objectively assess successMissing Context - Doesn't specify important constraintsUnrealistic - Can't be accomplished in the given scenario

searchDocs.title: Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before moving to Step 2, verify:   Intent states a clear, specific goal Success can be objectively measured Scope is appropriate (not too broad/narrow) Aligns with a failure mode category Uses clear, unambiguous language    Next: Step 2: System Prompt  TODO Content to Add: More detailed examples by failure mode categoryVideo walkthrough of intent creationIntent quality rubricCommon reviewer feedback on intents

searchDocs.title: Purpose of System Prompt​
searchDocs.content: The system prompt:  Defines the AI's role (persona, expertise level)Sets behavioral guidelines (tone, format, constraints)Grants tool permissions (web search, etc.)Provides context (background info, rules)

searchDocs.title: 1. Role/Persona Definition​
searchDocs.content: Who is the AI in this scenario?  Example:  You are a helpful research assistant with expertise in finding and summarizing current information.

searchDocs.title: 2. Behavioral Instructions​
searchDocs.content: How should the AI behave?  Example:  When responding: - Use clear, concise language - Cite sources for all factual claims - If uncertain, acknowledge limitations - Stay focused on the user's question

searchDocs.title: 3. Tool Permissions (if applicable)​
searchDocs.content: What tools can the AI use?  Example:  You have access to the following tools: - web_search: For finding current information online - news_search: For finding recent news articles Use these tools when the user asks about current events or recent information.

searchDocs.title: 4. Constraints and Requirements​
searchDocs.content: What are the boundaries?  Example:  Constraints: - Keep responses under 300 words - Always provide at least 2 sources - Use a professional, neutral tone

searchDocs.title: Do ✅​
searchDocs.content: Be specific and clear - Avoid ambiguous instructionsEnable the intent - Include everything needed to succeedUse consistent structure - Follow established templatesTest edge cases - Consider what could go wrong

searchDocs.title: Don't ❌​
searchDocs.content: Contradict yourself - No conflicting rulesOver-constrain - Don't make task impossibleUnder-specify - Include necessary detailsAssume knowledge - Be explicit about what AI should do

searchDocs.title: Template Structure​
searchDocs.content: # Role You are [role/persona with relevant expertise]. # Guidelines When responding to users: - [Guideline 1] - [Guideline 2] - [Guideline 3] # Tools You have access to: - [tool_name]: [when/how to use] # Constraints - [Constraint 1] - [Constraint 2] # Format [Any specific formatting requirements]

searchDocs.title: Examples by Intent Type​
searchDocs.content: Web Search Intent # Role You are a research assistant helping users find current, accurate information. # Guidelines - Always search for recent information when asked about current events - Provide source citations for all factual claims - Summarize findings clearly and concisely - If search results are unclear, acknowledge uncertainty # Tools You have access to web_search for finding current information online. # Constraints - Responses should be 200-300 words - Include at least 2 source citations - Use objective, factual language   Instruction Following Intent # Role You are a helpful assistant designed to follow instructions precisely. # Guidelines - Read all instructions carefully before beginning - Complete each step in the order given - Adhere to all formatting and constraint requirements - Do not skip steps or make assumptions # Constraints - Follow the exact format specified by the user - Respect all word count or length requirements - Maintain the requested tone throughout

searchDocs.title: Issue 1: Doesn't Enable Intent​
searchDocs.content: Problem: System prompt doesn't give AI what it needs to fulfill intent.  Example:  Intent: Search the web for newsSystem Prompt: "You are helpful"Missing: Web search tool permission!

searchDocs.title: Issue 2: Contradictory Instructions​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Rules that conflict with each other.  Example:  "Be extremely detailed and thorough""Keep responses under 100 words"Conflict: Can't be both!

searchDocs.title: Issue 3: Vague Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Instructions that are unclear or subjective.  Example:❌ "Be reasonably concise" ✅ "Keep responses between 150-250 words"

searchDocs.title: Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before moving to Step 3, verify:   System prompt defines role/persona All necessary guidelines included Tool permissions specified if needed Constraints are clear and measurable No contradictory instructions Enables the intent defined in Step 1    Previous: Step 1: Intent | Next: Step 3: User Prompts  TODO Content to Add: Library of tested system prompt templatesCommon tool configurationsPersona examples librarySystem prompt testing guidelines

searchDocs.title: Purpose of User Prompts​
searchDocs.content: User prompts:  Trigger the scenario - Set up the situation for testingTest the intent - Require AI to demonstrate target behaviorProvide necessary context - Include information AI needsBe realistic - Reflect how real users communicate

searchDocs.title: 1. Natural and Realistic​
searchDocs.content: Should sound like something a real user would say.  ❌ "Execute the web search protocol for climate change data" ✅ "What are the latest developments in climate change policy?"

searchDocs.title: 2. Complete Context​
searchDocs.content: Include all information the AI needs without assuming prior knowledge.  ❌ "What happened next?" (What does "next" refer to?) ✅ "After the 1969 moon landing, what was NASA's next major achievement?"

searchDocs.title: 3. Appropriate Complexity​
searchDocs.content: Not too simple, not unnecessarily complex.  Too Simple: "Hi" Too Complex: Multi-paragraph scenario with 10 sub-questions Just Right: Clear question with relevant context

searchDocs.title: 4. Tests the Intent​
searchDocs.content: Should require the AI to demonstrate the behavior you're testing.

searchDocs.title: Pattern 1: Direct Request​
searchDocs.content: Straightforward question or request.  Example:  Can you search for recent news about renewable energy adoption in Europe and summarize the key trends?

searchDocs.title: Pattern 2: Multi-Part Request​
searchDocs.content: Multiple related elements in one prompt.  Example:  I'm researching electric vehicles. Can you: 1. Find the latest sales figures 2. Identify the top 3 manufacturers 3. Highlight any recent technological breakthroughs

searchDocs.title: Pattern 3: Contextual Request​
searchDocs.content: Includes background/context before the request.  Example:  I'm writing a report on space exploration. I need to include information about recent Mars missions. What have been the most significant discoveries in the last year?

searchDocs.title: Pattern 4: Constraint-Heavy Request​
searchDocs.content: User specifies additional requirements.  Example:  Can you find information about artificial intelligence in healthcare? Please keep it under 200 words and include at least two recent examples.

searchDocs.title: Multi-Turn Scenarios​
searchDocs.content: For intents involving conversation continuity:

searchDocs.title: Turn 1 (Setup)​
searchDocs.content: Can you help me create a weekly meal plan?

searchDocs.title: Turn 2 (Continuation)​
searchDocs.content: Great! Now can you adjust that plan to be vegetarian?

searchDocs.title: Turn 3 (Further Refinement)​
searchDocs.content: And can you add a grocery list for those meals?   Key: Each turn should build on previous context.

searchDocs.title: Issue 1: Assumes Missing Context​
searchDocs.content: Problem: References information not provided.  ❌ "What else did he accomplish?" (Who is "he"?) ✅ "What other accomplishments did Albert Einstein have besides relativity?"

searchDocs.title: Issue 2: Artificial or Contrived​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Doesn't sound like real user communication.  ❌ "Initiate web search protocol for topic X and output results in format Y" ✅ "Can you look up recent information about topic X for me?"

searchDocs.title: Issue 3: Too Complex​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Testing multiple things at once.  ❌ Three-paragraph backstory + 5 different questions + specific formatting requirements ✅ Focus on one clear request with necessary context

searchDocs.title: Issue 4: Grammar/Spelling Issues​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Unintentional errors (unless testing error handling).  Note: Real users make typos, but for training data, keep prompts clean unless specifically testing the AI's handling of errors.

searchDocs.title: Examples by Intent Type​
searchDocs.content: Web Search Intent Good Examples: "What's the current weather forecast for Tokyo?""Can you find recent news about the Mars rover?""What are experts saying about the new climate report?" Bad Examples: "Search" (too vague)"Use web_search tool to find..." (too technical)"What's the answer?" (no context about what)  Instruction Following Intent Good Examples: "Write a 200-word summary of photosynthesis in simple terms""Create a list of 5 healthy breakfast ideas, formatted as a numbered list""Explain quantum computing to a 10-year-old in one paragraph" Bad Examples: "Write something" (no constraints)Instructions with contradictions built inPrompts that don't test the specific instructions

searchDocs.title: Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before moving to Step 4, verify:   Prompt sounds natural and realistic All necessary context included Tests the intent appropriately No assumed knowledge or missing context Appropriate complexity level Grammar and spelling correct (unless intentionally testing errors) For multi-turn: each turn builds logically on previous    Previous: Step 2: System Prompt | Next: Step 4: Bad Response  TODO Content to Add: User prompt library by categoryMulti-turn conversation templatesComplexity assessment guideReal vs. artificial prompt comparison examples

searchDocs.title: Purpose of Good Response​
searchDocs.content: The good response should:  Fulfill the intent - Accomplish what was requestedFollow system prompt - Adhere to all instructionsAvoid the failure - Not exhibit the bad response's problemDemonstrate best practices - Show ideal AI behavior

searchDocs.title: Characteristics of a Good Good Response​
1. Directly Addresses the User Prompt​
searchDocs.content: Answers what was asked clearly and completely.

searchDocs.title: 2. Follows All Instructions​
searchDocs.content: Adheres to:  System prompt guidelinesFormatting requirementsConstraints (word count, tone, etc.)Tool usage expectations

searchDocs.title: 3. Avoids the Failure Mode​
searchDocs.content: Specifically does NOT have the problem shown in the bad response.

searchDocs.title: 4. Natural and Appropriate​
searchDocs.content: Human-like tone (not robotic)Appropriate formality levelWell-structured and clear

searchDocs.title: 5. Complete and Accurate​
searchDocs.content: Provides sufficient detailFactually correct (when verifiable)Doesn't leave obvious questions unanswered

searchDocs.title: Must Have:​
searchDocs.content: Directly addresses the user's request Follows system prompt instructions Respects all stated constraints Avoids the bad response's failure mode Natural, human-like tone Appropriate level of detail

searchDocs.title: Should Have:​
searchDocs.content: Well-organized structure Clear, concise language Helpful and informative Professional quality

searchDocs.title: Must NOT Have:​
searchDocs.content: The failure mode from bad response Violations of system prompt Obvious errors or omissions Overly robotic language

searchDocs.title: Examples​
searchDocs.content: Instruction Retention - Good Response System Prompt: Always cite sources for factual claims. Keep responses under 200 words. User Prompt: What caused the fall of the Roman Empire? Good Response: The fall of the Roman Empire resulted from multiple interconnected factors [1]. Military pressures from Germanic tribes along the borders weakened Rome's defenses throughout the 4th and 5th centuries [2]. Economic troubles, including inflation and heavy taxation, strained the empire's resources [1]. Political instability and frequent leadership changes undermined effective governance [3]. The empire's vast size made centralized control increasingly difficult, while corruption eroded civic institutions [2]. The split between Eastern and Western empires in 285 CE further weakened Western Rome [1]. These factors culminated in 476 CE when the last Western Roman Emperor was deposed [3]. Sources: [1] History.com - Fall of Roman Empire [2] Britannica - Roman Empire Decline [3] Ancient History Encyclopedia - End of Western Rome Word count: 142 Why it's good: Stays under 200 wordsIncludes 3 sources cited throughoutAnswers the question thoroughlyWell-organized and clear  Tool Usage - Good Response System Prompt: Use web_search when asked about current events. User Prompt: What's happening with the Mars rover right now? Good Response: Let me search for the latest information about the Mars rover. [Performs web_search] Based on current reports, NASA's Perseverance rover is currently exploring the Jezero Crater delta region on Mars. Recent updates indicate: - The rover successfully collected its 20th rock sample last week - Scientists are particularly excited about samples showing signs of ancient organic compounds - The Ingenuity helicopter completed its 65th flight, setting new distance records - The team is preparing for the next phase focusing on sedimentary rock formations These activities are part of the mission's goal to search for signs of ancient microbial life and collect samples for potential return to Earth. Source: NASA Mars Mission Updates (accessed today) Why it's good: Actually uses web_search tool as instructedProvides current, specific informationCites sourceWell-structured and informative

searchDocs.title: Contrast with Bad Response​
searchDocs.content: The good response should make the bad response's failure obvious by comparison.

searchDocs.title: Example: Instruction Retention​
searchDocs.content: Aspect	Bad Response	Good ResponseWord count	400+ words	Under 200 words ✓ Citations	None	3 sources cited ✓ Following instructions	❌	✅  The contrast should be clear and immediate.

searchDocs.title: Mistake 1: Still Contains Failure​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Good response has traces of the bad response's failure.  Example:  Bad: Ignores word limit completely (500 words)"Good": Slightly exceeds word limit (220 words when 200 requested)Issue: Should clearly stay within limit

searchDocs.title: Mistake 2: Over-Corrects​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Goes so far the other direction it creates new issues.  Example:  Bad: No sources cited"Good": 20 citations for a simple answerIssue: Should be appropriate, not excessive

searchDocs.title: Mistake 3: Too Perfect/Robotic​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Doesn't sound natural or human-like.  Example:  "As per your request, I shall provide information regarding the topic of inquiry. Pursuant to the system parameters, I am maintaining adherence to the specified word count limitation..."   Issue: Too formal and mechanical

searchDocs.title: Mistake 4: Doesn't Answer Question​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Follows instructions but doesn't address user's actual need.  Example:  User: "What's the weather in Tokyo?""Good": Explains how weather forecasting works (following system prompt to be educational)Issue: Should answer the actual question!

searchDocs.title: Balancing Quality and Realism​
searchDocs.content: Good responses should be:  High quality - Demonstrate best practicesRealistic - What a good AI would produceNot perfect - Don't need to be absolutely flawlessAppropriate - Match the context and user need

searchDocs.title: Quality Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before moving to Step 6, verify:   Fulfills the intent completely Follows all system prompt instructions Clearly avoids the failure mode Natural, human-like tone Appropriate level of detail Well-structured and clear Factually accurate (when applicable) Creates clear contrast with bad response    Previous: Step 4: Bad Response | Next: Step 6: Review Checklist  The Gold Standard Your good response is the example we want the AI to learn from. Make it excellent!  TODO Content to Add: Good response library by categoryQuality benchmarks and rubricsNatural vs. robotic language examplesCommon reviewer feedback on good responses

searchDocs.title: Purpose of Bad Response​
searchDocs.content: The bad response should:  Demonstrate a clear failure - Show specific problematic behaviorBe realistic - Could plausibly come from an AITeach by contrast - Highlight what NOT to doMatch a failure mode - Align with defined categories

searchDocs.title: Characteristics of a Good Bad Response​
1. Clear, Obvious Failure​
searchDocs.content: The problem should be immediately apparent, not subtle.  Obvious: AI completely ignores word count constraint (500 words when 100 requested) Too Subtle: AI uses 105 words when 100 requested

searchDocs.title: 2. Realistic​
searchDocs.content: Should be something an AI might actually produce.  Realistic: AI forgets an earlier instruction and violates constraint Unrealistic: "ERROR: SYSTEM_MALFUNCTION_404"

searchDocs.title: 3. Single Failure Mode​
searchDocs.content: Focus on one clear problem, not multiple issues.  Focused: AI forgets to cite sources (instruction retention) Unfocused: AI forgets sources AND gives wrong info AND uses wrong tone

searchDocs.title: 4. Demonstrates Specific Category​
searchDocs.content: Maps to a defined failure mode category.

searchDocs.title: Instruction Retention​
searchDocs.content: AI forgets or ignores earlier instructions.  Example:  System: Keep responses under 100 words User: Tell me about climate change Bad Response: [350-word essay that ignores the constraint]

searchDocs.title: Course Correction​
searchDocs.content: AI fails to adapt when corrected or when context changes.  Example:  User: Summarize this article in French AI: [Gives English summary] User: Please provide it in French as requested Bad Response: [Still gives English summary or says it can't]

searchDocs.title: Task Continuation​
searchDocs.content: AI loses context or restarts instead of building on previous work.  Example:  User: Help me plan a trip to Japan AI: [Provides initial suggestions] User: Great! Now add accommodation recommendations Bad Response: "Sure, where would you like to travel?" [Lost context]

searchDocs.title: Bad Response Template​
searchDocs.content: [Response that demonstrates the failure mode clearly] **Failure Mode:** [Name of specific failure mode] **Why it's bad:** [Brief explanation of the problem]

searchDocs.title: Examples​
searchDocs.content: Instruction Retention Failure System Prompt: Always cite sources for factual claims. Keep responses under 200 words. User Prompt: What caused the fall of the Roman Empire? Bad Response: The fall of the Roman Empire was caused by multiple factors including military defeats, economic troubles, and political instability. The empire faced increasing pressure from barbarian invasions along its borders. Internal corruption weakened the government's ability to respond effectively. The economy suffered from inflation and over-taxation. Additionally, the empire became too large to govern effectively... [continues for 400 words with NO citations] Failure: Completely ignores requirement to cite sources.  Tool Misuse Failure System Prompt: Use web_search when asked about current events. User Prompt: What's happening with the Mars rover right now? Bad Response: Based on what I know, the Mars rover is conducting experiments on the Martian surface. It's exploring various geological features and collecting samples. [Makes up current details without searching] Failure: Should have used web_search but relied on outdated/fabricated info instead.

searchDocs.title: Mistake 1: Too Subtle​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Failure is hard to detect or requires careful reading.  ❌ AI uses slightly awkward phrasing ✅ AI completely ignores a clear constraint

searchDocs.title: Mistake 2: Unrealistic​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Wouldn't actually come from an AI.  ❌ Random gibberish or error codes ✅ Plausible but flawed reasoning

searchDocs.title: Mistake 3: Multiple Failures​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Demonstrates several problems at once.  ❌ Bad grammar + wrong info + ignores constraints + wrong tone ✅ One clear failure mode

searchDocs.title: Mistake 4: No Clear Failure​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Response is just mediocre, not clearly wrong.  ❌ Adequate but uninspired response ✅ Obvious violation of instructions or logic

searchDocs.title: Quality Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before moving to Step 5, verify:   Demonstrates ONE specific failure mode Failure is obvious and clear Could realistically come from an AI Violates intent or system prompt in clear way Failure mode category identified Not just "bad"—demonstrates specific problem    Previous: Step 3: User Prompts | Next: Step 5: Good Response  Reviewer Focus This is often where reviewers focus most. Make sure the failure is crystal clear! searchDocs.title: Purpose​
searchDocs.content: web_search enables the AI to search the internet for current, up-to-date information on any topic.

searchDocs.title: Good Use Cases ✅​
searchDocs.content: Current events: "What's happening with [recent event]?"Recent information: "What's the latest research on [topic]?"Fact verification: "Is it true that [claim]?"General research: "Find information about [topic]"Updates: "What are the current statistics on [metric]?"

searchDocs.title: Poor Use Cases ❌​
searchDocs.content: Historical facts: "When was the Roman Empire founded?" (Static knowledge)Definitions: "What does 'photosynthesis' mean?" (Standard knowledge)Math/logic: "What's 2+2?" (No search needed)Personal opinions: "What's your favorite color?" (Not searchable)

searchDocs.title: Basic Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to web_search for finding current information online.

searchDocs.title: Detailed Permission:​
searchDocs.content: You have access to the web_search tool. Use it when: - Users ask about current events or recent developments - You need up-to-date information to answer accurately - Users request you to "search" or "look up" information - Historical knowledge may be outdated When using web_search: - Cite sources from the results - Indicate you're searching before calling the tool - If results are unclear or limited, acknowledge this

searchDocs.title: Scenario 1: Current Events Query​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You are a helpful assistant with access to web_search for current information.   User Prompt:  What are the latest developments in renewable energy technology?   Good Response (Uses Tool):  Let me search for the latest information on renewable energy technology. [Calls web_search] Based on recent reports, key developments include: 1. **Solar Panel Efficiency**: New perovskite solar cells reaching 30%+ efficiency in lab tests [1] 2. **Battery Storage**: Solid-state batteries showing promise for grid-scale storage [2] 3. **Offshore Wind**: Floating wind farms being deployed in deeper waters [1] These advances are accelerating the transition to renewable energy sources. Sources: [1] MIT Technology Review - Recent renewable energy breakthroughs [2] Nature Energy - Battery technology advances (2024)   Bad Response (Doesn't Use Tool):  Renewable energy technology has been advancing in several areas including solar panels, wind turbines, and energy storage. Solar panels have become more efficient over the years, and wind energy is expanding globally... [Provides general/outdated knowledge without searching]

searchDocs.title: Scenario 2: Should NOT Use Tool​
searchDocs.content: System Prompt:  You are a helpful assistant with access to web_search for current information. Use web_search for current events and recent information, but rely on your base knowledge for historical facts and standard definitions.   User Prompt:  Who was the first president of the United States?   Good Response (Doesn't Use Tool):  George Washington was the first president of the United States, serving from 1789 to 1797. He was unanimously elected by the Electoral College and set many precedents for the office that continue today.   Bad Response (Unnecessarily Uses Tool):  Let me search for that information... [Calls web_search for basic historical fact] George Washington was the first president...

searchDocs.title: Issue 1: Hallucinated Search​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI claims to search but doesn't actually call the tool.  ❌ "I searched and found that..." [No actual tool call made] ✅ [Actually calls web_search tool] "Based on my search results..."

searchDocs.title: Issue 2: Wrong Tool Choice​
searchDocs.content: Problem: Uses web_search when news_search would be better.  User: "What's in the news about the election?" Better: Use news_search (more specific) Acceptable: Use web_search (works but less optimal)

searchDocs.title: Issue 3: Ignoring Tool Results​
searchDocs.content: Problem: AI calls tool but provides different information.  ❌ [Searches, gets results about X] "Based on my search, here's information about Y..." [Wrong] ✅ [Searches, gets results about X] "Based on my search, here's what I found about X..."

searchDocs.title: Creating Good Examples:​
searchDocs.content: Clear Need: User prompt should obviously benefit from current informationSystem Permission: System prompt explicitly grants web_search accessTool Indicator: Good response should show the search happeningCitation: Good response should cite search resultsRealistic: Tool usage should match real AI behavior

searchDocs.title: Example Test:​
searchDocs.content: **Intent:** Test appropriate use of web_search for current information **System Prompt:** You are a helpful assistant with web_search access. Use it for current information and recent events. **User Prompt:** What's the current status of the James Webb Space Telescope mission? **Bad Response (Tool Misuse):** The James Webb Space Telescope was launched in December 2021 and is designed to observe the universe in infrared. It will study galaxy formation, star formation, and exoplanets. [Uses outdated launch information, doesn't search for current status] **Good Response (Proper Tool Use):** Let me search for the latest information on the James Webb Space Telescope. [Calls web_search] As of the most recent updates, the James Webb Space Telescope: - Successfully completed its commissioning phase - Has been transmitting groundbreaking images of distant galaxies - Recently discovered organic molecules in a distant exoplanet atmosphere - All 18 mirror segments and instruments are operating nominally The mission has exceeded initial expectations in terms of image quality and scientific output. Source: NASA JWST Mission Updates, [current date]     Back to: Tools Overview  Citation Required When using web_search, always cite the sources from results. This shows proper tool result handling. searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Guideline Ambiguity​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Reviewer and submitter interpret a guideline differently, both interpretations seem reasonable.  Resolution Approach:  Identify which interpretation better serves training goalsMake a ruling for this caseDocument the clarificationFlag guideline for update  Example:  Guideline says "clear failure mode"Reviewer says failure must be in first sentenceSubmitter says failure can be anywhere if it's obviousDecision: Failure must be clear but doesn't require specific placement; document this clarification

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Guidelines Don't Cover This​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submission uses an approach not mentioned in guidelines—not prohibited, just not documented.  Resolution Approach:  Evaluate on core principles (training value, clarity, consistency)If valuable and not harmful → AcceptDocument as precedentRecommend guideline addition

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: "Good Enough" Debate​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Reviewers disagree on whether quality meets the bar.  Resolution Approach:  Apply rubric objectivelyCheck critical elements are presentIf meets minimum bar → AcceptIf multiple weaknesses compound → Reject  Ruling Framework:  Single minor issue + otherwise strong → AcceptMultiple minor issues → RejectAny critical issue → Reject

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Exceptional but Flawed​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submission excels in most areas but has one notable flaw.  Resolution Approach:  Is the flaw in a critical area (intent, failure mode clarity)? Yes → Reject with feedbackNo → Consider accepting with note Can the flaw be easily fixed? Consider revision opportunity

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Novel Failure Mode​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submitter demonstrates a failure mode not in the standard categories.  Resolution Approach:  Is it a real, observable AI failure?Is it clear and teachable?Could it fit an existing category with clarification?  Decision Matrix:  Real + Clear + Novel → Accept + flag for category considerationReal + Unclear → Reject, ask for clarityNot realistic → Reject

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Experimental Format​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submission uses a non-standard structure but includes all required elements.  Resolution Approach:  Are all critical elements present?Is it as clear as standard format?Does it add value or just difference?  Default: If equally effective → Accept. If reduces clarity → Reject.

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Lost Context​
searchDocs.content: Situation: User prompts or responses reference previous conversation context that isn't shown.  Resolution Approach:  Is missing context critical to understanding? Yes → Reject, request complete contextNo → Accept with note Can a reviewer understand the scenario without it?  Rule of Thumb: A fresh reviewer should understand everything without external information.

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Implicit vs. Explicit​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Intent or failure mode is implied but not explicitly stated.  Resolution Approach:  If 95% of reviewers would catch it → AcceptIf requires careful reading or inference → Reject for explicitness  Example:  Implicit: System prompt says "be concise" and bad response is 500 words → Clear enoughImplicit: Bad response has subtle logical error → Too implicit, reject

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Minimal Changes on Resubmission​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submitter makes tiny changes after rejection and resubmits.  Resolution Approach:  Did they address the feedback? Fully → Re-evaluate fairlyPartially → Reject with specific remaining issuesNot at all → Reject + note about feedback adherence Don't penalize for small changes if they fix the issue

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Disagreement with Rejection​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Submitter appeals, claiming reviewer misunderstood.  Resolution Approach:  Re-read submission objectivelyWas reviewer's assessment accurate?Was feedback clear and correct?  Possible Outcomes:  Reviewer was right → Uphold, explain whyReviewer missed something → Overturn, clarify for futureLegitimate gray area → Use tie-breaking principles

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Pattern of Issues from Submitter​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Same submitter repeatedly makes similar mistakes.  Resolution Approach:  Judge this submission on its own meritsNote pattern separately for ops teamDon't punish current submission for past issuesConsider additional training/feedback for submitter

searchDocs.title: Edge Case: Changing Standards​
searchDocs.content: Situation: Guidelines or precedents have evolved; submission would have passed before.  Resolution Approach:  Apply current standardsBe explicit about what changedProvide clear guidance on current expectationsConsider grandfather clause only if explicitly stated

searchDocs.title: When to Escalate Further​
searchDocs.content: Even adjudicators sometimes need to escalate:  Escalate to Ops Team when:  Guidelines are fundamentally unclear or contradictoryDecision would set major new precedentSystemic issue affecting multiple submissionsProposed guideline change needed    Learn from Every Case Each edge case you resolve adds to the collective knowledge. Document them well!  TODO Content to Add: Actual anonymized case studiesDecision statistics by categoryPrecedent database/searchable archiveCommon appeal outcomes

searchDocs.title: Intent (Step 1)​
searchDocs.content: Intent is clear and specific Success criteria are measurable Scope is appropriate (not too broad/narrow) Aligns with failure mode categories No ambiguous language

searchDocs.title: System Prompt (Step 2)​
searchDocs.content: Defines role/persona clearly Includes all necessary instructions Specifies tool permissions (if needed) States constraints clearly No contradictory rules Enables the intent

searchDocs.title: User Prompt(s) (Step 3)​
searchDocs.content: Natural and realistic Complete context provided Tests the intent appropriately No assumed missing information Appropriate complexity Proper grammar/spelling (For multi-turn) Logical flow between turns

searchDocs.title: Bad Response (Step 4)​
searchDocs.content: Demonstrates ONE specific failure mode Failure is obvious and clear Realistic (could come from AI) Matches defined failure mode category Not multiple failures mixed together Not nonsensical or artificial

searchDocs.title: Good Response (Step 5)​
searchDocs.content: Fulfills the intent completely Follows all system prompt instructions Clearly avoids the failure mode Natural, human-like tone Appropriate level of detail Factually accurate (when applicable) Well-structured and clear Creates obvious contrast with bad response

searchDocs.title: Consistency Check​
searchDocs.content: System prompt enables what intent requires User prompt tests what intent specifies Bad response violates intent or system prompt Good response fulfills both intent and system prompt All components work together coherently

searchDocs.title: Quality Standards​
searchDocs.content: Meets minimum quality bar for training data Would be useful for model learning Clear educational value (teaches good vs. bad) No confusing or contradictory elements

searchDocs.title: Technical Requirements​
searchDocs.content: All required fields completed Proper formatting throughout Links/references work (if applicable) Metadata fields filled (effective date, owners) Follows template structure

searchDocs.title: Things Submitters Often Forget​
searchDocs.content: Citations in Good Response If system prompt requires sources, good response must include them! Tool Usage If system prompt grants tool access and user prompt needs it, responses should reflect tool use Constraint Violations Bad response should clearly violate a constraintGood response should clearly respect all constraints Context Continuity In multi-turn scenarios, check that context flows naturally Failure Mode Documentation Note which specific failure mode the bad response demonstrates

searchDocs.title: Self-Review Questions​
searchDocs.content: Ask yourself:

searchDocs.title: Intent & Design​
searchDocs.content: Why did I choose this intent?What specific failure mode am I testing?How does this help model training?

searchDocs.title: Clarity​
searchDocs.content: Could a new reviewer understand this without explanation?Is the failure mode immediately obvious?Is the contrast between good and bad clear?

searchDocs.title: Realism​
searchDocs.content: Would a real user ask this way?Could an AI actually produce this bad response?Does the good response sound natural?

searchDocs.title: Completeness​
searchDocs.content: Have I included all necessary context?Are there any assumptions I'm making?Could this submission stand alone?

searchDocs.title: Final Quality Assessment​
searchDocs.content: Rate your submission honestly:  Aspect	Self-AssessmentIntent clarity	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ System prompt quality	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ User prompt realism	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Bad response clarity	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good response quality	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Overall coherence	⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐  Minimum bar: All aspects should be at least ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (4/5)  If anything is below 4 stars, revise before submitting.

searchDocs.title: Green Lights ✅​
searchDocs.content: All checklist items verifiedAll components are high qualityClear contrast between good and badCoherent end-to-end flowMeets training data needs

searchDocs.title: Red Flags 🚩​
searchDocs.content: Any checklist item failedUnclear or ambiguous elementsSubtle or questionable failure modeMissing context or informationWould confuse reviewers

searchDocs.title: Post-Submission​
searchDocs.content: After submitting:  Review Feedback Carefully If rejected, read reviewer comments thoroughlyAddress all points raisedAsk for clarification if needed Learn from Patterns Track common feedback you receiveAdjust your approach accordinglyReview guidelines for those areas Iterate and Improve Use feedback to get betterRefer back to examplesAsk questions when unsure    Previous: Step 5: Good Response  Pro Tip Print this checklist or keep it open in a second window while creating submissions! searchDocs.title: Your Role​
searchDocs.content: As an Adjudicator, you resolve complex cases, edge scenarios, and reviewer disagreements. You're the final decision-maker who ensures consistency and interprets guidelines when situations aren't clear-cut.

searchDocs.title: Key Responsibilities​
searchDocs.content: ⚖️ Resolve Disputes - Make final decisions on reviewer disagreements🔍 Handle Edge Cases - Evaluate submissions that don't fit standard patterns📋 Interpret Guidelines - Apply guidelines to novel situations📊 Ensure Consistency - Set precedents for future similar cases🎓 Educate - Help reviewers understand complex decisions

searchDocs.title: When Cases Come to You​
searchDocs.content: Submissions reach adjudication when:  Reviewer Uncertainty - Reviewer can't confidently decideReviewer Disagreement - Multiple reviewers have conflicting opinionsNovel Scenarios - Situation not covered by current guidelinesAppeal - Submitter contests a rejectionQuality Audit - Random sampling for calibration

searchDocs.title: 1. Review the Context​
searchDocs.content: What's the specific question or disagreement?What have reviewers already said?What guidelines apply?

searchDocs.title: 2. Evaluate Against Standards​
searchDocs.content: Does it meet the core quality bar?Is it useful for model training?Does it align with project goals?

searchDocs.title: 3. Consider Precedent​
searchDocs.content: Have we seen similar cases?What decisions were made before?Should this set a new precedent?

searchDocs.title: 4. Make a Clear Decision​
searchDocs.content: Provide definitive ruling (accept/reject)Explain reasoning thoroughlyNote if this creates a new precedent

searchDocs.title: Decision Principles​
searchDocs.content: Core Principles Consistency First - Similar cases should have similar outcomesIntent Over Form - Focus on whether submission achieves its purposeTraining Value - Would this improve model performance?Clarity - When in doubt, favor clear over clever

searchDocs.title: Impact of Your Decisions​
searchDocs.content: Your rulings:  Set precedents for future casesGuide reviewer calibrationShape interpretation of guidelinesInfluence project direction

searchDocs.title: Your Role​
searchDocs.content: As a Reviewer, you ensure submission quality by evaluating whether submissions meet project standards. Your feedback shapes the quality of the training data and helps submitters improve.

searchDocs.title: Key Responsibilities​
searchDocs.content: 🔍 Evaluate Submissions - Assess completeness, clarity, and alignment with guidelines💬 Provide Constructive Feedback - Explain issues clearly and suggest improvements✅ Accept or Reject - Make clear decisions based on established criteria📊 Maintain Consistency - Apply rubrics uniformly across all submissions

searchDocs.title: What You're Evaluating​
searchDocs.content: Intent Quality - Is it clear, measurable, and appropriately scoped?System Prompt - Does it enable the intent without contradictions?User Prompts - Are they realistic and well-contextualized?Bad Response - Does it demonstrate a clear, realistic failure mode?Good Response - Does it meet the intent and avoid the failure?

searchDocs.title: Decision Framework​
searchDocs.content: Your Decision Should Be Based On: Does this submission meet the quality bar?Would this be useful for training an AI model?Are all required components present and correct?Is the failure mode clear and realistic?

searchDocs.title: Common Review Scenarios​
searchDocs.content: ✅ Accept - All criteria met, minor or no issues❌ Reject with Feedback - Fixable issues that need submitter revision🤔 Edge Case - Escalate to adjudicator if unclear

searchDocs.title: Impact of Your Work​
searchDocs.content: Your reviews directly impact:  Training data qualitySubmitter learning and improvementOverall project successAI model performance

searchDocs.title: Next Steps​
searchDocs.content: Study the Rubric and Checklist for evaluation criteriaLearn How to Write Feedback effectivelyReview Common Edge Cases for tricky situations    TODO Content to Add: Sample review workflow with screenshotsExpected review time per submissionCalibration examples (borderline cases)Reviewer performance metrics

searchDocs.title: 1. "Almost There" Submissions​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Submission is 90% good but has 1-2 minor fixable issues.  Guidance:  If issues are truly minor (typos, small formatting): Consider accepting with a noteIf issues affect quality or clarity: Reject with specific feedbackAsk yourself: "Would this confuse model training?" If yes, reject.  Example:  Small typo in user prompt → Likely acceptMissing a required citation in good response → Reject for revision

searchDocs.title: 2. Creative but Off-Template Submissions​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Submitter used a creative approach that doesn't match standard templates but might be valid.  Guidance:  Evaluate if it still meets the intent and quality barDon't reject solely for being different from examplesIf unsure whether creative approach is acceptable → Escalate to adjudicator  Example:  Non-standard system prompt structure but all elements present → Evaluate on meritCompletely novel failure mode demonstration → Escalate for adjudication

searchDocs.title: 3. Subtle Failure Modes​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: The bad response's failure is subtle or requires careful reading to detect.  Guidance:  Failure modes should be obvious, not requiring detective workIf you have to re-read multiple times to find the failure → RejectSubmitter should add clearer contrast or explanatory note  Example:  Bad response violates a constraint buried in system prompt → Ask for more obvious failureFailure only visible if you count exact words → Too subtle, reject

searchDocs.title: 4. Multiple Potential Failures in Bad Response​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Bad response demonstrates 2-3 different failure modes simultaneously.  Guidance:  Best practice: One clear failure mode per submissionMultiple failures can confuse trainingAsk submitter to isolate one specific failure  Example:  Bad response both forgets instructions AND provides wrong information → Reject, ask to pick one

searchDocs.title: 5. Disagreement with Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Submission follows your interpretation but conflicts with a guideline detail.  Guidance:  Guidelines take precedence over personal interpretationRefer to specific guideline sections in feedbackIf guideline is unclear → Note this when escalating

searchDocs.title: 6. Unclear Whether Failure Is Realistic​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: You're not sure if the bad response represents a real AI failure or is artificially constructed.  Guidance:  Ask: "Could a language model plausibly produce this?"Nonsensical gibberish → Not realistic, rejectPlausible but wrong reasoning → Realistic, likely acceptIf truly unsure → Escalate with your reasoning

searchDocs.title: 7. Good and Bad Responses Very Similar​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: You have to look closely to see the difference between good and bad responses.  Guidance:  The contrast should be immediately apparentIf difference is too subtle → Reject and ask for clearer distinctionProvide specific suggestion for how to make contrast clearer

searchDocs.title: 8. Borderline Quality​
searchDocs.content: Scenario: Submission meets minimum bar but isn't great; you're unsure if it's "good enough."  Guidance:  Use the rubric weights to guide decisionIf all critical elements are present and correct → Likely acceptIf multiple "barely acceptable" areas → Consider rejecting for overall qualityDocument your reasoning for consistency

searchDocs.title: When to Escalate to Adjudicator​
searchDocs.content: Escalate when:  You've spent significant time and still can't decideGuidelines don't clearly cover the scenarioCreative/novel approach might be valuable but you're uncertainMultiple reviewers might reasonably disagree  Escalation Template:  Context: [Brief description of submission] Issue: [What makes this an edge case] My Analysis: [Your thoughts and concerns] Question: [Specific question for adjudicator]     Build Your Judgment These cases get easier with experience. When in doubt, err on the side of quality and document your reasoning. searchDocs.title: The Decision Hierarchy​
searchDocs.content: When evaluating edge cases, apply these principles in order:

searchDocs.title: 1. Training Value (Highest Priority)​
searchDocs.content: Question: Will this submission improve model training?  If yes → Lean toward acceptanceIf no/unclear → Lean toward rejectionConsider: Is the signal clear enough to teach the model?  Example:  Subtle failure mode that's hard to detect → Low training value → RejectClear failure with obvious contrast → High training value → Accept

searchDocs.title: 2. Guideline Alignment​
searchDocs.content: Question: Does this align with documented guidelines?  If explicitly allowed → AcceptIf explicitly prohibited → RejectIf unclear → Move to next principle  Example:  Novel but guideline-compliant approach → AcceptCreative but violates stated requirements → Reject

searchDocs.title: 3. Consistency with Precedent​
searchDocs.content: Question: How have we handled similar cases?  If consistent precedent exists → Follow itIf conflicting precedents → Re-evaluate and set new standardIf no precedent → Set one and document  Example:  Previous similar creative approaches accepted → Accept this onePast decisions rejected this pattern → Reject unless circumstances differ

searchDocs.title: 4. Intent Over Form​
searchDocs.content: Question: Does it accomplish the core purpose?  If purpose is clearly achieved → Accept despite non-standard formIf purpose is not met → Reject even if form is correct  Example:  Non-standard template but all elements present and clear → AcceptPerfect template but unclear failure mode → Reject

searchDocs.title: 5. Clarity and Simplicity​
searchDocs.content: Question: Is it unambiguous and straightforward?  When all else is equal, favor simpler and clearerComplexity should add value, not confusion  Example:  Two acceptable approaches: choose the clearer oneComplex scenario without added value → Ask for simplification

searchDocs.title: Scenario A: Borderline Quality​
searchDocs.content: 50/50 case - barely meets vs. barely misses the bar  Default: If critical elements are present → Accept Rationale: Small improvements can be made in iteration; blocking potentially useful data is costly  Exception: If multiple borderline areas → Reject and ask for overall quality improvement

searchDocs.title: Scenario B: Novel Approach​
searchDocs.content: Creative submission that doesn't match examples  Default: If it achieves intent and isn't prohibited → Accept Rationale: Innovation can be valuable; don't penalize creativity  Exception: If novelty introduces ambiguity → Reject or ask for clarification

searchDocs.title: Scenario C: Reviewer Disagreement​
searchDocs.content: One reviewer accepts, one rejects  Process:  Review both perspectives thoroughlyIdentify the core disagreementApply decision hierarchy aboveDocument reasoning for future reference  Default: Side with the reviewer who provides more specific, guideline-based reasoning

searchDocs.title: Scenario D: Submitter Appeal​
searchDocs.content: Submitter contests rejection  Process:  Review original submission and rejection feedbackReview submitter's appeal reasoningEvaluate if reviewer missed something or misapplied guidelines  Default: Support reviewer unless clear error in judgment or guideline application  Common Valid Appeals:  Reviewer missed a key element that was presentGuideline was misinterpreted by reviewerPrecedent exists that supports acceptance  Common Invalid Appeals:  "I worked hard on this" (not relevant to quality)"Other submitters do this" (without evidence)Disagreement with the guidelines themselves

searchDocs.title: Documentation Requirements​
searchDocs.content: When making an adjudication decision:

searchDocs.title: Always Document:​
searchDocs.content: The specific question/disagreementYour decision and rationaleWhich principles appliedWhether this sets a precedent

searchDocs.title: Template:​
searchDocs.content: **Case:** [Brief description] **Question:** [Specific point of contention] **Decision:** Accept / Reject **Rationale:** [Explanation using principles above] **Precedent:** Sets new precedent / Follows existing / Case-specific **Guidance for Future:** [Any clarification for similar cases]     Golden Rule When truly stuck, ask: "What decision serves the project's goal of high-quality training data?" Then make the call.  TODO Content to Add: Real adjudication case studiesDecision tree flowchartCommon disagreement patternsAppeals process details

searchDocs.title: 1. Be Specific​
searchDocs.content: ❌ "The intent isn't clear enough" ✅ "The intent should specify whether the AI should search for news articles or academic papers"

searchDocs.title: 2. Be Actionable​
searchDocs.content: ❌ "Bad response needs work" ✅ "The bad response should demonstrate instruction retention failure by having the AI forget the initial constraint about staying under 100 words"

searchDocs.title: 3. Be Constructive​
searchDocs.content: ❌ "This is completely wrong" ✅ "The system prompt doesn't enable web search. Try adding: 'You have access to web_search tool for finding current information'"

searchDocs.title: 4. Reference Guidelines​
searchDocs.content: ❌ "This doesn't follow the rules" ✅ "Per the Failure Modes guide, instruction retention failures should show the AI forgetting a specific constraint"

searchDocs.title: For Intent Issues​
searchDocs.content: The intent needs clarification on [specific aspect]. Consider: [specific suggestion or example] Reference: [link to relevant guideline if applicable]

searchDocs.title: For System Prompt Issues​
searchDocs.content: The system prompt is missing [specific element] needed to [accomplish what]. Suggestion: [concrete example of what to add]

searchDocs.title: For Response Quality Issues​
searchDocs.content: The [good/bad] response doesn't clearly demonstrate [expected behavior/failure]. Expected: [what should be shown] Current: [what is currently shown]

searchDocs.title: Do ✅​
searchDocs.content: Use neutral, professional languageAcknowledge what works wellFrame as opportunities for improvementAssume good intent from submitter

searchDocs.title: Don't ❌​
searchDocs.content: Use harsh or dismissive languageFocus only on negativesBe vague or unclearMake assumptions about submitter's effort or ability

searchDocs.title: Example Feedback (Good)​
searchDocs.content: Full Example: Rejecting a Submission Overall Assessment: Reject with revision opportunity Positive Notes: The intent scope is appropriate and the user prompt is well-formedSystem prompt structure follows the template correctly Issues to Address: Bad Response Failure Mode (Critical): The bad response doesn't clearly demonstrate a specific failure mode. Currently it just provides a short answer, but it's unclear if this is instruction retention, task continuation, or another failure. Please explicitly show one failure mode, such as the AI forgetting the requirement to "provide sources for all claims." Good Response Completeness (Major): The good response is missing source citations even though the system prompt requires them. Add at least 2-3 inline citations to demonstrate proper adherence to instructions. Minor Grammar (Minor): Small typo in the user prompt: "searchs" should be "searches" Next Steps: Please revise the bad response to clearly show instruction retention failure, add citations to the good response, and fix the typo. Happy to review again once updated!

searchDocs.title: Feedback Checklist​
searchDocs.content: Before submitting your review, verify:   I've identified specific issues with examples I've provided actionable suggestions for fixes I've linked to relevant guidelines where helpful My tone is constructive and professional I've noted positive aspects when present The submitter will understand what to change    Remember Your feedback is a teaching opportunity. The goal is to help submitters improve, not just to catch errors. searchDocs.title: Step 1: Intent Definition​

searchDocs.title: Step 2: System Prompt​

searchDocs.title: Step 3: User Prompts​
searchDocs.content: User prompts naturally test the intent Prompts are realistic and well-formed Sufficient context provided for the AI Edge cases or complexities included where appropriate

searchDocs.title: Step 4: Bad Response​
searchDocs.content: Demonstrates a specific, identifiable failure mode Failure is clear and obvious (not subtle) Response is realistic (could plausibly come from an AI) Failure mode documented in response notes

searchDocs.title: Step 5: Good Response​
searchDocs.content: Directly addresses the user prompt Follows system prompt instructions Avoids the failure mode shown in bad response Demonstrates best practices Natural and human-like in tone

searchDocs.title: Problem​
searchDocs.content: Intents that don't clearly define success or allow multiple interpretations.  ✅ Good Example Intent: "Provide a factual, cited answer to a specific historical question using web search" Clear success criteria and scope ❌ Bad Example Intent: "Help the user with their question" Too vague - what kind of question? What does "help" mean?

searchDocs.title: 2. System Prompts That Don't Enable the Intent​
Problem​
searchDocs.content: System prompt doesn't give the AI the tools or instructions needed to fulfill the intent.  ✅ Good Example Intent: Search the web for recent news System Prompt: "You are a helpful assistant with access to web search tools. When users ask about recent events, use the news_search tool to find current information." Includes tool permissions and usage guidance ❌ Bad Example Intent: Search the web for recent news System Prompt: "You are a helpful assistant." Missing tool permissions and search instructions

searchDocs.title: Problem​
searchDocs.content: Bad responses that are obviously terrible or wouldn't realistically come from an AI.  Common Error Creating bad responses that are completely nonsensical or include obvious errors like: Random gibberish"ERROR: System malfunction"Completely off-topic responses These don't represent real failure modes!

searchDocs.title: 4. Good and Bad Responses Too Similar​
Problem​
searchDocs.content: The difference between good and bad responses isn't clear or meaningful.  Best Practice The contrast should be obvious to reviewers. If you have to explain the difference in detail, the examples probably aren't distinct enough.

searchDocs.title: Problem​
searchDocs.content: User prompts that assume information not provided in the conversation.  See Example Bad: "What happened next?" (Reader doesn't know what "next" refers to) Good: "After the Apollo 11 landing in 1969, what was the next major milestone in the space program?"

searchDocs.title: Problem​
searchDocs.content: Creating unnecessarily complex edge cases that test multiple things at once.  Keep It Simple Focus on one clear failure mode per submission. Complex scenarios make it harder for reviewers and less useful for training. searchDocs.title: Your Role​
searchDocs.content: As a Submitter, you are responsible for creating high-quality task submissions that demonstrate proper AI behavior and failure mode examples. Your work forms the foundation of the training data used to improve AI models.

searchDocs.title: Key Responsibilities​
searchDocs.content: 📝 Craft clear, well-structured intents and prompts🤖 Generate appropriate system prompts that align with project goals✅ Create "good" responses that demonstrate ideal AI behavior❌ Create "bad" responses that illustrate specific failure modes🔍 Ensure all submissions meet quality standards before review

searchDocs.title: Success Criteria​
searchDocs.content: Your submissions should:  Be Clear and Unambiguous - Intents and prompts should have a single, obvious interpretationFollow Template Structures - Use established patterns for system prompts and responsesDemonstrate Real Failure Modes - Bad responses should represent actual problematic behaviorsInclude Complete Context - Provide all necessary information for reviewers

searchDocs.title: What Makes a Good Submission?​
searchDocs.content: Key Traits Clear intent that defines success criteriaSystem prompt that enables the desired behaviorUser prompts that test the AI appropriatelyBad response that demonstrates a specific failure modeGood response that shows the correct behavior

searchDocs.title: Overall Quality Bar​
searchDocs.content: A submission should be accepted if:  All required components are presentQuality meets or exceeds minimum standardsNo critical issues that would confuse model trainingMinor issues (if any) don't undermine the core value  A submission should be rejected if:  Missing or incomplete required componentsQuality below minimum standardsContains confusing or contradictory elementsFailure mode is unclear or unrealistic

searchDocs.title: 1. Intent (Weight: 20%)​
searchDocs.content: Clarity: Intent is unambiguous and has clear success criteria Scope: Appropriately sized (not too broad or narrow) Alignment: Matches project failure mode categories Testability: Can be objectively evaluated  Red Flags:  Vague language like "help the user" without specificsMultiple different interpretations possibleConflicting goals within the intent

searchDocs.title: 2. System Prompt (Weight: 25%)​
searchDocs.content: Completeness: Includes all necessary instructions Clarity: Instructions are clear and unambiguous Consistency: No contradictory rules Enablement: Gives AI what it needs to fulfill intent Tool Permissions: Appropriate tool access defined  Red Flags:  Missing critical instructionsContradictory guidelinesDoesn't enable the intentOverly restrictive or permissive

searchDocs.title: 3. User Prompts (Weight: 15%)​
searchDocs.content: Realism: Natural, realistic user inputs Context: Sufficient information provided Relevance: Tests the intent appropriately Clarity: Well-formed and grammatically correct  Red Flags:  Assumes information not in contextArtificially complex or contrivedPoor grammar (unless intentional for testing)

searchDocs.title: 4. Bad Response (Weight: 20%)​
searchDocs.content: Clear Failure: Demonstrates obvious failure mode Realistic: Could plausibly come from an AI Specific: Matches a defined failure mode category Documented: Failure mode noted/explained  Red Flags:  Failure is subtle or ambiguousCompletely nonsensical (unrealistic)Multiple different failures mixed togetherUnclear what failure mode is being shown

searchDocs.title: 5. Good Response (Weight: 20%)​
searchDocs.content: Addresses Intent: Fulfills the stated goal Follows Instructions: Adheres to system prompt Avoids Failure: Doesn't exhibit the bad response's failure Natural: Human-like and appropriate tone Complete: Fully answers the user prompt  Red Flags:  Doesn't actually address the promptViolates system prompt instructionsStill contains elements of the failure modeOverly robotic or unnatural

searchDocs.title: Quick Decision Guide​
searchDocs.content: Scenario	Decision	ActionAll criteria met, no issues	✅ Accept	Approve with positive note Minor fixable issues	❌ Reject	Specific, constructive feedback Major fundamental problems	❌ Reject	Detailed explanation of issues Unclear/borderline case	🤔 Escalate	Send to adjudicator with context    Calibration Tip When in doubt, compare to example submissions in the reference section.
